act_now.title: canonicalize_json_value cannot detect duplicate keys; safety depends entirely on the caller also running raw canonicalize() first
act_now.detail: canonicalize_json_value()/from_serde_value() build the preimage from a serde_json::Value whose Map has already collapsed duplicate object members (last-wins), so this path structurally CANNOT enforce P-CANON-DUPKEY-REJECT. It is a public, re-exported crate-root function (lib.rs:38) and is the function signing_preimage() uses (signing.rs:104). Today the pipeline happens to also call canonicalize(raw_bytes) at step 3 before computing the preimage (pipeline.rs:164/245), which closes the gap — but that ordering is the ONLY thing preventing a dup-key message from being signed/verified over a canonicalized-but-deduplicated preimage. The dup-key invariant lives in a different function than the one that produces the signed bytes; any future caller that builds a preimage via canonicalize_json_value without first running the raw path on the original wire bytes silently bypasses duplicate-key rejection. The doc comment warns of this but the type system does not enforce it. Recommend the preimage path consume the validated JcsValue from the raw parse rather than re-deriving from serde, so a single parse both rejects dupes and produces the canonical bytes.

act_now.title: x-mcp-header-promoted routing header is never validated or re-checked against the signed body
act_now.detail: validate_routing_headers (called from routing_header_rejection in tls.rs:410) only iterates [MCP_METHOD_HEADER, MCP_NAME_HEADER]. The SEP-2243 x-mcp-header-promoted header is neither subjected to the strict single-valued/well-formed hygiene rules (P-V03-ROUTING-HEADERS-UNTRUSTED rule 4) NOR re-validated against the signed body value (P-V03-PROMOTED-ARG-REVALIDATE). The catalog requires that for any security-relevant promoted argument the node verify the signed body value and re-check the header against it, failing closed on mismatch. The proxy strips/ignores the MCP-S envelope and routes solely on the signed body, so routing is benign, but the explicit promoted-header revalidation/anti-smuggling property is not enforced at all. Spec/code drift against ADR-MCPS-025: the promoted header is admitted without any of the strict-header treatment its sibling routing headers receive.

act_now.title: XFCC-derived asserted identity is not length-bounded or control-char validated (strict-header rules applied only to the plain path)
act_now.detail: `parse_xfcc` extracts the policy-selected field (URI/DNS/CN) and only rejects empty values (lines 422-426). Unlike `parse_plain` (line 356), it never runs the extracted value through `validate_asserted_identity_value`, so an XFCC-forwarded identity is NOT length-bounded (MAX_ASSERTED_IDENTITY_LEN, 8192) and NOT control-character checked. ADR-MCPS-023 (P-V03-ASSERTED-HEADER-STRICT) requires Tier-2 asserted-identity metadata to be well-formed (no control chars), length-bounded (oversized fails closed) and single-valued. The conflict/duplicate rules are enforced, but well-formed+length-bounded are silently skipped for the XFCC format. RFC2253 unescaping (`unescape_rfc2253_value`, line 556) can even reintroduce a control char from a `\<char>` escape that survived the line-based header parse. Impact: an oversized or control-char-bearing identity from a compromised/misconfigured upstream becomes the bound transport identity (used in ExactMatchBinding string compare and emitted to logs), enabling unbounded log/identity payloads and weakening the anti-smuggling posture the plain path enforces.

act_now.title: Reverse-proxy asserted-identity trust is purely operator-asserted with no authenticated LB↔node channel gate
act_now.detail: ReverseProxyMtlsProvider::verified_identity reads the configured trusted header and, after dup/shape checks, returns it verbatim as a *verified* TransportIdentity that the binding policy then treats as authoritative. ADR-MCPS-023 (P-V03-INGRESS-AUTH-IS-GATE) requires that ALL asserted-identity metadata be treated as attacker-controlled UNLESS the immediate peer is authenticated as a configured trusted ingress over an authenticated LB↔node channel; network location / header presence is explicitly NOT sufficient. Here the only gate is a CLI opt-in flag (main.rs:368) plus a loud startup warning (main.rs:101) — there is no code that authenticates the upstream peer (no peer-cert check, no allowlisted source, no mTLS on the LB↔node link) before trusting the header. Anyone who can reach the listening socket and set the configured header name spoofs any signer-bound identity. The module doc (lines 278-290) and CLI surface this as an operator assertion, so it is a documented residual-risk design rather than a silent fail-open, but the property requires the gate be enforced, not asserted, and a stronger claim (Tier-2 trusted ingress) is effectively made without the authenticated-channel precondition being machine-checked.

act_now.title: verify_response omits batch/notification rejection (asymmetric with verify_request)
act_now.detail: verify_response (pipeline.rs:236-284) never calls reject_batch or reject_notification, unlike verify_request (steps 1-2). The normative verify_response order in P-FAILCLOSED-RESPONSE-ORDER does not list batch/notification rejection, so this is arguably spec-conformant; however a top-level JSON array supplied as a 'response' is parsed as a Value, passes canonicalize(), then extract_response_envelope() does value.get("result") which returns None on an array -> MissingEnvelope. It still fails closed, but reports mcps.missing_envelope rather than mcps.batch_forbidden. Not exploitable (fails closed) but the error token is misleading and the response path is structurally weaker than the request path. Flag for reviewer confirmation against core-spec verify_response steps.

act_now.title: deserialize_envelope error classification depends on serde_json human-readable 'unknown field' prefix
act_now.detail: deserialize_envelope (constraints.rs:194-203) distinguishes UnknownEnvelopeField from CanonicalizationFailed by string-matching err.to_string().starts_with("unknown field"). This couples the frozen error taxonomy to serde_json's display wording. The code mitigates this with a pinning test (serde_wording_pins_guard / serde_unknown_field_wording_is_pinned) that fails CI if the phrasing changes, which is the right guard, but the mechanism is inherently brittle: a serde_json bump that rephrases would silently route every unknown-field violation to CanonicalizationFailed (still fail-closed, but wrong token, and trust_label-on-response would no longer be reported as unknown_envelope_field) until the pinning test caught it. Acceptable given the guard; flagged as a residual coupling for the reviewer.

act_now.title: check_signature_block does not reject empty signature.value string (present-but-empty)
act_now.detail: check_signature_block (step 7) only rejects alg != Ed25519 and value.is_none(); a present-but-empty value ("") passes step 7. It will later fail at verify_ed25519 (step 11) since an empty base64url decodes to a zero-length signature and Ed25519 verify fails -> InvalidSignature. So it still fails closed, but the intended cheap-check rejection of a malformed signature.value is incomplete; the format validity of signature.value (Base64URL-no-pad, 64-byte length) is deferred entirely to crypto. Relative to P-SIGN-ENCODING this means encoding validity is not enforced as a distinct fail-closed step. Net effect is fail-closed, hence low.

act_now.title: UnknownEnvelopeField classification depends on serde_json human-readable 'unknown field' prefix
act_now.detail: deserialize_envelope (line 197) discriminates UnknownEnvelopeField solely by err.to_string().starts_with("unknown field"). Per P-UNKNOWN-FIELD-FORBIDDEN any non-frozen envelope field MUST yield mcps.unknown_envelope_field. This mapping is brittle: a serde_json version bump that rephrases the message silently downgrades unknown-field rejections to CanonicalizationFailed. The code DOES fail closed (still an error, never accept), and a pinning test (serde_wording_pins_guard) exists in this file to fail CI on rephrase, so risk is mitigated; flagged low because the conformance verdict token (not the accept/reject decision) is wording-coupled. trust_label-in-response rejection (P-UNKNOWN-FIELD-FORBIDDEN's MUST-NOT) rides on this same fragile path.

act_now.title: Preimage/request_hash built from serde Value (last-wins) but duplicate-key safety relies on prior raw-bytes step ordering
act_now.detail: request_signing_preimage and request_hash (lines 193, 215) operate on the serde_json::Value, which cannot represent duplicate object members (serde Map is last-wins). Duplicate-key rejection lives only on the raw-bytes path canonicalize(raw_bytes) at step 3 (line 164). The Value-path is therefore safe ONLY because step 3 unconditionally runs before envelope extraction/preimage construction and rejects any duplicate-key input first. This is correct as written, but it is a load-bearing ordering invariant with no defense-in-depth: if a future refactor reordered or made step 3 conditional, a duplicate-key payload could reach the last-wins Value path and produce a preimage over a different member than a naive parser saw, enabling a canonicalization/signature confusion. The same coupling exists in verify_response (canonicalize at line 245 before response_signing_preimage at 261). Recommend an explicit comment-enforced or test-enforced invariant tying step 3 to the Value-derived preimage. Maps to the dup-key/JCS-before-sig properties.

act_now.title: Duplicate-key detection is structurally coupled to a separate raw-bytes pass; the serde value path silently cannot enforce P-CANON-DUPKEY-REJECT
act_now.detail: canonicalize_json_value / from_serde_value operate on serde_json::Value, whose Map silently keeps the last of duplicate keys, so this path CANNOT detect duplicate members (documented at lines 107-120). Duplicate-key rejection therefore depends ENTIRELY on the caller having first run the raw-bytes canonicalize(raw_bytes) over the original wire bytes. The in-repo request/response pipelines do this (pipeline.rs:164/245 run canonicalize(raw_bytes) at step 3 before any serde-derived preimage), so P-CANON-DUPKEY-REJECT holds today. The risk is conformance-by-convention: canonicalize_json_value is a pub crate-root export with no compile-time guarantee that a raw-bytes dup-key check preceded it. Any future signing/hashing caller that builds bytes via canonicalize_json_value on serde values WITHOUT a prior raw pass (e.g. manifest.rs:121, signing.rs:104 — both currently fed already-verified values) would canonicalize a last-wins reduction of a duplicate-key object and bypass the dup-key MUST. Worth a guard or a doc-enforced invariant test asserting the pipeline never reaches a serde preimage without the raw pass.

act_now.title: Trace-context stripping is limited to the container _meta; trace keys in nested _meta blocks remain in the signing scope
act_now.detail: strip_observability_meta only removes traceparent/tracestate/baggage from object[container_key]._meta (params._meta for requests, result._meta for responses). MCP permits _meta at multiple nesting levels (e.g. params.arguments._meta, or per-content-block _meta inside result.content[]). W3C Trace Context can legitimately be carried at those inner _meta levels too, and a tracing middle box that rewrites a nested traceparent would change the signed preimage and break an otherwise-valid signature. Per ADR-026 the exclusion targets 'the located _meta' (the container), so this is arguably spec-faithful, but the contract that 'mutating a trace field MUST NOT break the signature' (P-META-OBSERVABILITY-UNSIGNED-NONDECISIONAL) only holds for the container-level _meta. If the deployment ever places trace context in a nested _meta this becomes a verification-availability bug. Confirm the located-_meta definition is intended to be container-only and document the constraint.

act_now.title: Observability-key exclusion is name-based inside the located _meta, allowing a decision-influencing field to be smuggled out of signing scope if any downstream layer reads traceparent/tracestate/baggage
act_now.detail: strip_observability_meta unconditionally removes the three OBSERVABILITY_META_KEYS from the located container's _meta before canonicalization. This correctly keeps trace fields out of the preimage (P-SIGN-PREIMAGE-EXCLUSIONS / P-META-OBSERVABILITY). The residual conformance risk is the dual obligation in P-META-OBSERVABILITY-UNSIGNED-NONDECISIONAL: these keys are now UNSIGNED, so any code path (in mcps-host/mcps-proxy/mcps-policy) that reads a key literally named traceparent/tracestate/baggage from params._meta would be acting on attacker-mutable, unsigned data. mcps-core enforces only the exclusion half; nothing in this unit guarantees the non-decisional half. Within core this is sound, but the partition's safety depends on downstream layers never reading these names — worth a cross-unit assertion/test.

act_now.title: Observability-key exclusion is unconditional and name-based, so a renamed-collision or consumer that reads params._meta.traceparent reads attacker-controlled unsigned data
act_now.detail: strip_observability_meta() unconditionally removes the three W3C trace keys (traceparent/tracestate/baggage) from the located container's _meta before canonicalization, regardless of value type or content. This is correct per ADR-026 (they must be out of signing scope), but it also means any value placed under exactly those three names is silently dropped from the integrity-protected preimage. The exclusion is purely name-based at the top-level container _meta. The security obligation that these keys MUST NOT influence any decision lives entirely in downstream consumers — nothing in core-signing prevents a future consumer from reading params._meta.traceparent and treating it as trusted. Flagging as a latent signing-scope hazard: the strip side is implemented, the 'and must not influence decisions' half is unenforceable here and must be guaranteed at every consumer.

act_now.title: Replay trait cannot reject non-positive remaining TTL before consulting the store (no now_unix passed)
act_now.detail: ReplayCache::check_and_insert receives only expires_at_unix, never now_unix. ADR-MCPS-020 (P-V03-REPLAY-TTL-ROUNDUP) requires a backend to compute remaining TTL from now, round it UP, and reject a non-positive remaining TTL BEFORE the shared store is consulted. With this signature a production (e.g. Redis) ReplayCache has no value to derive remaining TTL from and cannot perform the 'already-stale -> reject before store' check at the cache boundary; it can only set an absolute expiry from expires_at_unix+skew. The in-memory reference is safe because freshness is checked earlier in the pipeline, but the trait as frozen pushes a normative responsibility onto callers who lack the input. Worth widening the trait (pass now_unix) or documenting that the backend must obtain its own now and round up.

act_now.title: Inner object result is signed in place without scrubbing a forged nested se.syncom/mcps.* block
act_now.detail: For an OBJECT inner result, build_signed_response clones it verbatim (proxy.rs:361) and only overwrites result._meta[RESPONSE_META_KEY] (proxy.rs:381). A hostile inner server can embed a forged se.syncom/mcps.verified (or other se.syncom/mcps.* block) inside result._meta (or deeper) and it becomes part of the SIGNED response the proxy vouches for. The sole-writer rule is correctly enforced on the request->inner path (VERIFIED_META_KEY removed at proxy.rs:314); this is the response-direction analogue. Non-decisional for the client (which trusts the response envelope, not an embedded verified block), so low, but the inner-injected MCP-S identifier blocks are not scrubbed from the result the proxy signs despite the inner being untrusted elsewhere in this file.

act_now.title: Caller-supplied *.verified block is stripped only from params._meta, not from other _meta locations
act_now.detail: build_forwarded_request removes VERIFIED_META_KEY (and REQUEST_META_KEY, AUTHORIZATION_META_KEY) exclusively from request["params"]["_meta"] (310-322). A caller who places a se.syncom/mcps.verified block at a different location the inner server might read (e.g. a top-level _meta, or nested under arguments) is not stripped by this code. The proxy is the SOLE writer of the verified block per ADR-MCPS-008 / P-VERIFIED-CONTEXT-SOLE-WRITER; relying on the inner server reading ONLY params._meta.verified is an implicit contract. For a well-behaved MCP inner that only consults params._meta this is safe, but the strip is location-specific rather than a recursive purge, so a future inner reading verified context elsewhere could be confused by an attacker-supplied copy. Recommend documenting/asserting the single canonical location or purging recursively.

act_now.title: Reverse-proxy identity-header ingress trusts a spoofable header on operator assertion only
act_now.detail: resolve_identity returns the identity from a forwarded header (ReverseProxyHeader strategy, 347) with no cryptographic proof the immediate peer is the trusted ingress; trust rests entirely on the operator asserting the socket is reachable only by the upstream (documented loudly in main.rs:99-112 and transport.rs:278-296, and rejected under --strict in cli.rs:978-985). This matches P-V03-INGRESS-AUTH-IS-GATE's warning that network location / header presence is not sufficient: the code provides NO LB-to-node channel authentication, only the operator gate. It is a documented, opt-in, strict-rejected posture rather than a silent fail-open, so severity is low; but per P-V03-INGRESS-AUTH-IS-GATE the node still treats the asserted identity as trusted without authenticating the immediate peer, which is the residual risk the property targets.

act_now.title: end_to_end_mtls mode does not reject asserted-identity headers; mode selection is single-flag, not mutually-exclusive-enforced
act_now.detail: P-V03-TIER2-EXPLICIT-OPTIN requires that a proxy in end_to_end_mtls (direct-TLS) mode IGNORE/REJECT asserted-identity metadata, and that header presence MUST NOT cause Tier-2. Selection is done by reverse_proxy_identity_header being Some/None (tls.rs:345-348, main.rs:368): DirectTls calls connection_identity and ignores headers, ReverseProxyHeader reads the header. The mutual exclusion is correct for identity *extraction*, but in DirectTls mode the asserted-identity header (e.g. x-forwarded-client-cert) is neither stripped nor rejected — it is simply not read for identity. If any downstream consumer (or a future routing/log path) reads that header, a Tier-1 deployment silently carries attacker-controlled asserted-identity metadata. Recommend that DirectTls mode actively reject (or strip) a present reverse-proxy identity header so presence can never leak into a security or observability decision.

act_now.title: DelegatedResponseSigner accepts arbitrary alg-agnostic sign callback with no Ed25519 enforcement
act_now.detail: DelegatedResponseSigner::new takes an opaque Box<dyn Fn(&[u8]) -> Result<String,KeyError>> and a VerificationKey with no check that the callback actually produces an Ed25519 signature. P-SIGN-ALG-ED25519-ONLY requires every KeySource tier sign with Ed25519. The pairing/algorithm correctness is delegated entirely to the caller (doc says 'The caller is responsible for the pairing'). For the in-tree reference impl the callback is genuine Ed25519, so this is a contract-by-comment rather than enforced invariant; a future HSM adapter wiring a non-Ed25519 device would not be caught here. Verifier-side the response signature is independently Ed25519-verified, so a mismatched alg fails closed at verify, limiting impact. Flagging as borderline spec/code-drift: the signer tier does not itself enforce the algorithm.

act_now.title: Reference profile parses the artifact for evaluation via serde_json::from_slice (last-wins), not the JCS dup-key-rejecting path
act_now.detail: evaluate_inner decodes the artifact with `serde_json::from_slice(artifact_bytes)` and then builds the signature preimage from that parsed Value (verify_issuer_signature clones `artifact`, removes signature.value, and canonicalize_json_value's the resulting Value). serde_json silently accepts duplicate object members (last-wins), so the Value used for signature verification AND for binding checks (grantee/subject/audience/scope) is the collapsed form, never the raw bytes. The only dup-key defense is upstream in PolicyEvaluator::evaluate_inner, where `expected_authorization_hash` calls mcps_core::canonicalize(artifact_bytes) (raw-bytes path) before the hash-binding compare. If that core canonicalize rejects duplicate keys, a dup-key artifact is caught at hash binding; if it does NOT (or if a profile is ever invoked without the evaluator's pre-check, e.g. ReferenceProfile::authorize called directly as the tests do), the artifact is interpreted via last-wins semantics. The profile should itself parse through the JCS-safe/dup-rejecting path rather than relying on the caller having already canonicalized.

act_now.title: Issuer-signature preimage is canonicalized from a re-serialized Value, not from the hash-bound artifact bytes
act_now.detail: verify_issuer_signature recomputes the signing preimage by cloning the from_slice-parsed Value, removing signature.value, and re-running canonicalize_json_value. This is a second, independent canonicalization of an already-parsed Value rather than a transform of the original artifact_bytes. It is correct only if canonicalize(artifact_bytes) and canonicalize_json_value(from_slice(artifact_bytes)) are guaranteed byte-identical for every input that passes hash binding. Any JCS-domain value that survives from_slice but that core's raw-bytes canonicalizer would treat differently (number/precision/escaping/UTF-8 edge cases) creates a gap between what was hash-bound and what is signature-verified. Lower risk because mint_reference_grant also signs canonicalize_json_value output, so well-formed issuers agree; flagged as a consistency/spec-gap that should derive the preimage from the bound bytes.

act_now.title: Reference profile parses the signed artifact with serde_json (last-wins) instead of the dup-key-rejecting canonical parse before verifying the issuer signature
act_now.detail: evaluate_inner does `serde_json::from_slice(artifact_bytes)` then `serde_json::from_value` into ReferenceGrant, and verify_issuer_signature re-canonicalizes that already-parsed serde Value (signature.value removed) via canonicalize_json_value. serde_json silently applies last-wins on duplicate object members. The dup-key REJECTION only happens transitively in expected_authorization_hash (which calls canonicalize on the raw bytes). The evaluator enforces hash-binding before authorize(), so a dup-key artifact whose raw bytes don't canonicalize is rejected as AuthorizationMalformed at hash time — closing the hole in the evaluator path. BUT ReferenceProfile::authorize is a public trait method that can be (and in tests is) invoked directly without the evaluator's prior canonicalize check; on that path a duplicate-key artifact is parsed last-wins and signature-verified over a re-canonicalized value, so the signed preimage the verifier checks need not equal the bytes that were hashed. Profiles should canonicalize the raw artifact bytes themselves rather than rely on the evaluator having done it. Maps to P-CANON-DUPKEY-REJECT / P-CANON-FAILCLOSED-BEFORE-SIG: the JCS-safe-domain + dup-key check must precede signature verification on every path, not only through the evaluator.

act_now.title: InMemoryRevocationSource is a non-persistent reference deny-list; its always-available contract means a process restart silently loses all revocations
act_now.detail: InMemoryRevocationSource::revocation_status always returns Ok(...) and never RevocationUnavailable, by design (an in-memory set is always 'available'). For the reference/non-persistent source this is correct as a reference, but it means a deployment that mistakenly wires the in-memory source as its real revocation backend gets a deny-list that resets to empty on every restart — every previously-revoked grant becomes NotRevoked (allow) with no operational-unavailable signal to flag the gap. This is an in-memory reference per the unit description, so it is expected behavior, but the 'always Ok / never unavailable' contract is exactly the property that makes a stale/empty store indistinguishable from a correct one. The P-V03-TRUST-WINDOW-FAILCLOSED-style bounded-staleness concern has no analogue here: a wiped in-memory revocation set fails OPEN (allow), not closed. Flagging low so the funnel records that the reference source must never back a production revocation decision and that there is no max-staleness / freshness guard on revocation state.

act_now.title: Reference profile verifies the issuer signature over a duplicate-key-blind canonicalization (serde Value path), not the raw-bytes JCS-safe path
act_now.detail: evaluate_inner does serde_json::from_slice(artifact_bytes) -> Value, and verify_issuer_signature then signs/verifies over canonicalize_json_value(preimage_value). Per mcps-core/src/canonical.rs:108-120, canonicalize_json_value CANNOT detect duplicate object members (serde_json::Map silently keeps last-wins); only the raw-bytes canonicalize()/parse() path enforces P-CANON-DUPKEY-REJECT. The reference profile never runs the raw-bytes parse over the artifact itself. The only thing that saves it is that the PolicyEvaluator computes expected_authorization_hash via canonicalize(artifact_bytes) (the raw path) BEFORE calling authorize (evaluator.rs:86), so a dup-key artifact is rejected as AuthorizationMalformed at the hash-binding step. That mitigation is external to the profile: any caller invoking ReferenceProfile::authorize directly (as the unit tests do) bypasses dup-key rejection entirely, and the profile trait doc even says implementations may assume artifact_bytes hashes to authorization_hash. The profile's own signature-verification preimage is therefore not JCS-domain-validated in-house. Recommend the profile run canonicalize() (raw path) on artifact_bytes before deriving the serde Value, mirroring Core's fail-closed-before-sig ordering.

act_now.title: Reference profile does not check the artifact's embedded `profile` field against the selecting block profile id
act_now.detail: The minted artifact embeds a top-level profile: REFERENCE_PROFILE_ID (reference.rs:318), but ReferenceGrant deserialization (reference.rs:67-78) ignores it and authorize never asserts artifact.profile == REFERENCE_PROFILE_ID. Profile selection is driven solely by the block.profile selector in the _meta block (evaluator.rs:79-82). Because authorization_hash binds the exact artifact bytes, this is not directly exploitable today, but it means a single signed artifact body could be presented under a different profile selector without the profile noticing a mismatch between the selector and the artifact's self-declared profile. Low-severity defense-in-depth gap; add an equality check so the artifact's self-identification must agree with the dispatching selector.

act_now.title: verify_response runs no batch/notification rejection; a top-level array response yields MissingEnvelope not a batch verdict
act_now.detail: verify_response (lines 236-284) omits reject_batch/reject_notification. A top-level JSON array passed to verify_response parses, passes canonicalize() if JCS-safe, then extract_response_envelope -> locate_envelope(msg,"result",..) calls .get("result") on an array, returning None -> MissingEnvelope. The normative response order P-FAILCLOSED-RESPONSE-ORDER does not enumerate batch/notification rejection for responses, so this is spec-conformant; flagged only because P-BATCH-FORBIDDEN's intent (reject arrays explicitly) is not surfaced on the response path and the verdict differs (MissingEnvelope vs batch_forbidden). Fails closed either way.

act_now.title: P-META-OBSERVABILITY / signing-preimage trace-key exclusion not enforced in this unit; verify relies on signing.rs preimage builders
act_now.detail: The pipeline builds preimages via request_signing_preimage/response_signing_preimage (lines 193, 261) and computes request_hash via request_hash (line 215). P-SIGN-PREIMAGE-EXCLUSIONS and P-META-OBSERVABILITY-UNSIGNED-NONDECISIONAL (exclude exactly signature.value + traceparent/tracestate/baggage) are enforced inside signing.rs, not here, so this unit cannot guarantee those exclusions. ids.rs defines OBSERVABILITY_META_KEYS; the pipeline never reads them. This is correct layering (single shared preimage), but for conformance coverage note that verify_request/verify_response do not independently strip/ignore observability _meta — they trust the preimage builder. Any decision-influencing field outside the located _meta would be unsigned-but-trusted; P-META-SIGNED-OR-IGNORED depends entirely on the envelope being under the signed _meta, which it is.

act_now.title: Transport binding (P-TRANSPORT-BIND-SIGNER) is absent from the core pipeline by design — confirm enforced upstream
act_now.detail: verify_request takes only raw_bytes, resolver, replay, config, now_unix — there is no mTLS peer identity parameter, so P-TRANSPORT-BIND-SIGNER (bind verified signer to mTLS peer, fail closed on mismatch with transport_binding_failed) and P-TRANSPORT-NOT-REPLACE-SIG cannot be enforced here. This is consistent with P-CORE-PURE (no networking in core). Recorded so the audit confirms transport binding is enforced in the host/transport unit and that verify_request's verified_signer is actually compared to the peer identity there; if no upstream comparison exists, transport binding is dead.

act_now.title: verify_response performs no freshness or replay protection on response envelopes
act_now.detail: verify_response (steps 1-7) binds the response to the request only via request_hash equality (line 275) and signature verification. It does NOT check any issued_at/expires_at freshness window on the response, nor consult a ReplayCache for the response signature. A captured valid response could be replayed against any later verifier that holds the same expected_request_hash. Per MCPS_SPEC §9 the response pipeline is defined as exactly these 7 steps (response freshness/replay are out of the core response contract; the request_hash binding plus the per-request nonce on the request side provide the freshness coupling), so this appears intentional and spec-conformant. Flagged as info so a reviewer confirms the threat model deliberately omits standalone response freshness rather than it being an oversight.

act_now.title: request_hash format of response envelope is not validated, only compared for equality
act_now.detail: verify_response compares envelope.request_hash against expected_request_hash by raw string equality. It never validates that the inbound request_hash is well-formed 'sha256:<b64url-no-pad>' (unlike the request-side authorization_hash, which is sha256:-prefix checked at check_authorization_hash). This is safe in practice because expected_request_hash is always the locally recomputed sha256:-prefixed value from VerifiedRequest.request_hash, so any malformed inbound value simply fails the equality check and yields ResponseHashMismatch. No bypass; noted only because the asymmetry (format-checking authorization_hash but not request_hash) could mislead a future maintainer. Per P-SIGN-ENCODING the canonical hash format is enforced at the producer (sha256_hash_id); the verifier relying on equality is adequate.

act_now.title: Observability-meta strip is best-effort/infallible and only targets the located container _meta
act_now.detail: strip_observability_meta removes traceparent/tracestate/baggage only from object[container]['_meta'] (params/_meta for requests, result/_meta for responses) and is infallible (silently no-ops if _meta is absent or not an object). This matches P-SIGN-PREIMAGE-EXCLUSIONS: exactly the W3C trace keys under the located _meta are excluded; trace keys placed at any OTHER location (e.g. a top-level _meta, or nested deeper) remain IN signing scope and are integrity-protected, which is the fail-safe direction (mutating them would break the signature rather than being silently ignored). Signer and verifier share signing_preimage so exclusions are symmetric. No defect; recorded to document that the exclusion is correctly scope-narrowed and cannot be abused to smuggle an unsigned decision-influencing field, since only the three fixed observability keys are stripped and only at the one location.

act_now.title: JcsValue is a public hand-constructible enum; only the depth bound — not the type — prevents an out-of-domain value (unsafe integer, lone-surrogate-free but otherwise) from being canonicalized via canonicalize_value
act_now.detail: canonicalize_value(&JcsValue) enforces only the recursion-depth bound (write_value, lines 199-238); it does NOT re-validate the safe-integer range or string content of a hand-built JcsValue, since the type's invariant ('constructed only by parse', line 42) is by-convention, not enforced — the variants are pub and re-exported at the crate root. JcsValue::Integer is i64, so a caller could construct Integer(9_007_199_254_740_993) (> 2^53-1) and canonicalize_value would happily emit it, violating P-CANON-SAFE-INTEGERS for that entry path. In practice all in-repo callers reach canonicalize_value via parse or from_serde_value (both of which enforce check_safe_integer), so the property holds on every real path. Flagged as a latent domain-bypass surface on the public API: the safe-integer MUST is enforced at construction sites, not at the serialize choke point, so the public canonicalize_value alone is not a complete JCS-domain gate.

act_now.title: serde_json value path cannot detect duplicate keys; correctness depends entirely on the raw-bytes path running first
act_now.detail: canonicalize_json_value()/from_serde_value() operate on a serde_json::Value whose Map silently keeps last-wins duplicates, so this path structurally CANNOT enforce P-CANON-DUPKEY-REJECT. The module doc (lines 110-116) correctly states the raw-bytes parse() must run on the original wire bytes first. The pipeline does call canonicalize(raw_bytes) at pipeline.rs:164/245 before signature verification, so the invariant currently holds. The risk is residual: any future caller that derives a serde_json::Value and routes only through canonicalize_json_value (bypassing the raw path) would accept duplicate keys and a JCS-domain violation would pass undetected. This is a fragile dual-path contract enforced only by call-site discipline, not by the type system. Worth a code-level guard or a doc-test asserting the pairing. No active defect in this unit.

act_now.title: parse_hash_id collapses three distinct malformations (missing prefix, bad base64, wrong length) into one error
act_now.detail: parse_hash_id returns CanonicalizationFailed for missing/wrong prefix, undecodable body, and non-32-byte length alike. For request_hash binding (P-RESPONSE-HASH-BIND) the relevant outcome is response_hash_mismatch, computed by the comparison logic at the pipeline layer, not here; this helper's job is purely structural validation and returning a single structural error before any equality comparison is defensible. Noting that a malformed request_hash identifier therefore fails as canonicalization_failed rather than response_hash_mismatch, which is the correct fail-closed precedence (structural validity before semantic comparison) but should be confirmed against the verify_response ordering in pipeline.rs (outside this unit).

act_now.title: No response_hash helper symmetric with request_hash; response binding relies on caller recomputing request preimage hash
act_now.detail: request_hash() exists as a convenience wrapper (request_signing_preimage + sha256_hash_id) but there is no equivalent that, given a request object, yields the canonical request-preimage hash for response binding in one call; response verifiers must independently chain request_signing_preimage()->sha256_hash_id(). P-RESPONSE-HASH-BIND requires response.request_hash equal the locally verified request signing-preimage hash (SHA-256 of JCS bytes after signature.value removal, NOT the transmitted JSON). The building blocks here are correct and request_hash() does exactly that for requests, so this is only an ergonomics gap that slightly raises the risk a downstream caller hashes transmitted bytes instead of the preimage. Verify the host/transport response path uses request_signing_preimage-derived hashing, not a hash of raw wire bytes.

act_now.title: signing_preimage does not assert the located envelope contains an Ed25519 alg; alg/key_id are retained verbatim into the preimage with no value-domain check at the signing layer
act_now.detail: signing_preimage strips only signature.value and the trace keys, retaining signature.alg and signature.key_id (correct per P-SIGN-PREIMAGE-EXCLUSIONS). The Ed25519-only enforcement (P-SIGN-ALG-ED25519-ONLY) lives entirely in pipeline.rs (check_signature_block at line 172 / response alg check at line 251), not in signing.rs or crypto.rs. crypto::verify_ed25519 will happily verify bytes regardless of the declared alg string. This is a deliberate layering split and is enforced on the canonical pipeline path, but any caller invoking request_signing_preimage + verify_ed25519 directly (bypassing verify_request) gets no alg gate. Not a defect on the canonical path; flagged so the alg check is recognized as pipeline-only, not preimage-intrinsic.

act_now.title: signature.value removal does not detect or reject a duplicate 'value' member inside the signature object; relies on serde_json Value (last-wins) since dup-key detection is delegated to the raw-bytes canonicalize path
act_now.detail: strip_signature_value operates on a serde_json::Value (already last-wins collapsed), and signing.rs's own doc-comment (lines 26-29) explicitly states duplicate-key detection is NOT done here and belongs to canonicalize(raw_bytes) run by the pipeline on original wire bytes (confirmed at pipeline.rs:164, BEFORE preimage construction). So P-CANON-DUPKEY-REJECT is enforced on the canonical path. The residual concern: signing.rs is a public API (request_signing_preimage/response_signing_preimage are pub) usable without the dup-key guard; a direct caller that derives a Value from untrusted bytes via serde_json without the raw-bytes canonicalize pre-check would silently accept duplicate members. Defense-in-depth only; canonical pipeline is conformant.

act_now.title: signing_preimage strips only signature.value; extra signature.* sub-keys remain in the preimage and rely entirely on the typed deny_unknown_fields step running first
act_now.detail: strip_signature_value() operates on the raw serde_json::Value and removes ONLY the 'value' member of the signature object (line 138). Any other member injected under signature (e.g. signature.injected) is retained in the canonical preimage. Safe today only because verify_request (pipeline.rs:167) runs extract_request_envelope -> deserialize_envelope -> SignatureBlock{deny_unknown_fields} at step 4-6, which rejects unknown signature sub-keys BEFORE the preimage is built at step 11. The signing.rs unit itself has no such guard, so the property 'preimage excludes exactly signature.value and nothing else' depends on an ordering invariant in a different unit. If any caller of signing_preimage/request_signing_preimage skips the typed envelope deserialization (e.g. host signer.rs builds the preimage directly from a json! value at signer.rs:115 with no deny_unknown_fields pass), a stray signature sub-key would silently become signed content. Documented as an inter-unit coupling risk, not an exploit in the current wiring.

act_now.title: signing_preimage clones and canonicalizes without enforcing the JCS-safe duplicate-key domain on its own input
act_now.detail: signing_preimage() builds the preimage via canonicalize_json_value(), which (per canonical.rs:117 docs) CANNOT detect duplicate object member names because it consumes a serde_json::Value whose Map already collapsed duplicates last-wins. Duplicate-key rejection is delegated to the raw-bytes canonicalize(raw_bytes) step run separately by the pipeline (pipeline.rs:164) BEFORE preimage construction. This is the documented design, and the request path is correct. The residual hazard is identical to the signature.* note: any signing_preimage caller that does not run the raw-bytes JCS domain check first (notably the host signer at signer.rs:115, which canonicalizes a freshly-built json! value it controls) gets no dup-key protection from this function. Benign for the host signer (it authors the object), but the function offers no defense-in-depth if reused on untrusted input.

act_now.title: Preimage location is fixed to top-level params/result _meta; trace-key exclusion does not reach nested _meta, which is correct but undocumented as a deliberate boundary
act_now.detail: strip_observability_meta only descends container_key()._meta (params._meta or result._meta). Trace keys appearing anywhere else (e.g. params.arguments._meta.traceparent, or a nested _meta) are NOT excluded and therefore remain signed. This is the correct reading of ADR-026 ('under the located _meta') and prevents an attacker from smuggling unsigned mutable regions into arbitrary nested positions, so it is a positive property. Recorded as info to confirm the exclusion scope was checked and is correctly minimal (no broad recursive trace-key stripping that would have widened the unsigned surface).

act_now.title: Trust resolution fail-closed mapping is complete and never falls back to allow
act_now.detail: Confirms conformance to P-TRUST-NEVER-FALLBACK-ALLOW: TrustResolverError maps NotFound/Revoked/MalformedKey -> ActorBindingFailed and Unavailable -> TrustResolverUnavailable, with no Ok-on-error path; the From impl agrees. UNAVAILABLE is kept distinct from a negative binding verdict (P-V03-TRUST-WINDOW-FAILCLOSED's distinct-error requirement). Note however that core defines NO trust-propagation window T enforcement (the 60s staleness ceiling of P-V03-TRUST-WINDOW-FAILCLOSED): bounded-TTL caching is delegated entirely to the out-of-crate resolver. That is consistent with P-TRUST-NO-CORE-REVOCATION-TRANSPORT (core must define no key-validity interval), so the absence is by design, not a gap — but the catalog's T-window fail-closed property is therefore UNENFORCEABLE within core and must be verified in the caching resolver implementation, which is outside this unit.

act_now.title: verify-time replay TTL round-up not enforced in core retention math (delegated to backend, out of pure scope)
act_now.detail: InMemoryReplayCache computes retain_until = expires_at_unix.saturating_add(max_clock_skew_secs). This is in-memory retention, not a shared-store TTL, so P-V03-REPLAY-TTL-ROUNDUP (round UP, reject non-positive remaining TTL before consulting the store) is not and cannot be enforced here. The trait check_and_insert takes only expires_at_unix and never receives now_unix, so a backend adapter implementing ReplayCache cannot reject an already-stale request before touching the store from this signature alone; it must re-derive remaining TTL externally. Worth confirming the backend adapter (mcps-host/transport) does the round-up + pre-store staleness reject, since the core trait does not surface now to enforce it.

act_now.title: Trust resolver fail-closed taxonomy correct; binding vs operational are distinct and neither maps to allow
act_now.detail: TrustResolverError maps NotFound/Revoked/MalformedKey -> ActorBindingFailed and Unavailable -> TrustResolverUnavailable; there is no allow/fallback branch. resolve returns Result with no default-key path. Satisfies P-TRUST-NEVER-FALLBACK-ALLOW for the core abstraction. Note: core defines no key-validity interval / revocation list (revocation = Binding::Revoked entry), consistent with P-TRUST-NO-CORE-REVOCATION-TRANSPORT.

act_now.title: Stale-active-trust propagation window T not representable in core resolver (delegated to caller cache)
act_now.detail: Module docs state bounded-TTL caching of resolver results is a caller concern and core neither caches nor enforces a key-validity interval. Consequently P-V03-TRUST-WINDOW-FAILCLOSED (serve cached active trust at most T, then fail closed with trust_resolver_unavailable) cannot be enforced in this unit; the resolver answer is treated as final at verify time. Confirm the caller-side cache enforces the max-T window and converts post-T unavailability into TrustResolverUnavailable rather than serving stale Active bindings indefinitely.

act_now.title: Host signs caller-supplied on_behalf_of / authorization_hash / audience without local format validation
act_now.detail: HostSigner::sign_request copies on_behalf_of, audience, and authorization_hash verbatim into the signed envelope with no check that authorization_hash matches sha256:<b64url> (P-REQUIRED-FIELDS / P-AUTHZ-HASH-BIND) or that on_behalf_of is non-empty. Acceptable by design — the host is the trusted signer and the verifier enforces these fail-closed (mcps.authorization_hash_missing / on_behalf_of_invalid_format), so this is NOT a verification bypass. Flagged info because a malformed value here yields a request that always fails remote verification (self-inflicted availability, not a security hole); a defensive pre-sign check would surface the error at the signer. No action required for the security boundary.

act_now.title: Verify-before-dispatch gate is correctly fail-closed; inner server unreachable on any verification failure
act_now.detail: handle_with_transport runs verify_request FIRST (216-225); on Err it returns json_rpc_error_object and never calls self.inner.dispatch (228-229). On Ok it runs Phase-5 authorization (233-248) and Phase-6 transport binding (251-255), each returning before dispatch_and_sign on failure. A replay-cache borrow failure maps to ReplayCacheUnavailable (224) rather than proceeding. This upholds P-AUTHZ-DENY-BEFORE-DISPATCH, P-TRANSPORT-BIND-SIGNER, P-FAILCLOSED-ORDER, P-REPLAY-CACHE-FAILCLOSED at the proxy seam. Reported as info (positive confirmation of the focus property).

act_now.title: build_signed_response signs every inner shape including inner errors and non-object results
act_now.detail: All four inner shapes (object result, scalar/array/null result, and no-result/inner-error) are normalized into a signed result object bound to verified.request_hash with the outgoing id taken from the verified request, not the inner-controlled id (359-399). A signer failure maps to ResponseSigInvalid and fails closed (394-397). This upholds P-VERIFY-EVERY-MESSAGE / P-RESPONSE-HASH-BIND for the response shape: a hostile inner cannot suppress the signature by returning a non-object or error. Positive confirmation.

act_now.title: connection_rejection + routing_header_rejection run after handshake and before handler (inner unreached)
act_now.detail: serve_once (529-534) and serve_connection (593-598) compute connection_rejection(...).or_else(routing_header_rejection) and only call handler when both return None. cert_lifetime_rejection fails closed on over-long, unparseable, inverted, and degenerate validity windows (leaf_cert_lifetime_secs returns None on not_after<=not_before, 358-370). routing_header_rejection maps SEP-2243 header duplicate/malformed to transport_binding_failed without ever routing on them (P-V03-ROUTING-HEADERS-UNTRUSTED). OCSP rejection is feature-gated and fail-closed on Revoked/Unknown/error (438-478). Confirms cert-lifetime and routing-header hygiene gates upheld.

act_now.title: fault_accept_any_client verifier is correctly feature-gated and off by default
act_now.detail: The AcceptAnyClientVerifier (which neuters client-cert trust/identity/expiry and makes client auth non-mandatory) is compiled ONLY under the non-default fault_accept_any_client feature (258-269, 941-1037); the default build path is the verifying WebPkiClientVerifier branch (271-277). This is the intended fault-injection control, not a production fail-open. Confirmed gated. Info.

act_now.title: SharedReplayCache passes now_unix=0 to the store; correctness depends entirely on every AtomicReplayStore impl ignoring it — a contract enforced only by convention/docs
act_now.detail: check_and_insert hands the store retain_until plus a hard-coded now_unix=0 (the vestigial anchor). The Redis store correctly reads its own clock and ignores the 0 (redis_store.rs:372 uses self.ttl_ms_for, not the passed 0), and the in-memory store ignores TTL entirely. But the trait signature still propagates the 0, so a future AtomicReplayStore implementor that naively trusts now_unix would reproduce the H-8/H-9 ~56-year-TTL DoS. This is a latent footgun in the trait contract rather than a live defect; the only guard is the doc comment on the trait method (lines 86-97). A type that cannot carry the wrong value (e.g. an explicit RetainUntil newtype with no now param) would be more conformance-robust.

act_now.title: BoundedTrustCache store() silently drops the write on a poisoned mutex but the read path treats poison as Unavailable — asymmetry is safe but worth noting
act_now.detail: cached() (read) maps a poisoned cache mutex to Some(Err(Unavailable)) = fail closed (lines 184-191), which is correct. store() (write) silently drops the entry on a poisoned mutex (lines 203-211). The asymmetry is safe: a dropped write just means the next request re-resolves the inner resolver (fail closed if the source is then down), never serving stale-active beyond T. No P-V03-TRUST-WINDOW-FAILCLOSED violation; the never-indefinite-stale property is upheld because active state lives at most t_secs and an Unavailable inner result is never cached (line 256). Recorded as info confirming the fail-closed posture is intact on both paths.

act_now.title: ReplayDurabilityTier::parse strict-production minimum is enforced by a separate caller, not at parse time
act_now.detail: meets_strict_production_minimum() correctly returns true only for RedisWaitQuorum/Linearizable, satisfying P-V03-REPLAY-TIER-CEILING's 'strict/production requires REDIS_WAIT_QUORUM or stronger'. However this is a pure predicate; the actual refuse-to-start gate that calls it is outside this unit (cli/startup). This unit cannot itself guarantee the strict-mode refusal happens — flagged info so the full audit confirms a startup caller actually invokes meets_strict_production_minimum() and aborts when false. No defect within the reviewed files.

act_now.title: PKCS#11 sign path does not bind the signing key to an authorized key-set / audience admission document
act_now.detail: find_key locates the Ed25519 private/public key purely by CKA_LABEL on the token and signs with it; the public key is read from CKA_EC_POINT. This is the signer side and is correct for custody (private key never leaves the device, exactly-one-object-or-fail-closed). P-V03-AUTHORIZED-KEYSET is a VERIFIER-side property (the verifier must trust an explicit key_id->audience admission doc, not a discovered key list) and lives elsewhere, so this is not a violation in this unit. Noting only that the response_public_key() exported here is the value a verifier must later admit via an authorized key-set; nothing in this file asserts the key_id/audience binding, which is correct layering. Informational, no action in this unit.

act_now.title: OCSP online revocation transport lives in mcps-proxy under a feature flag, not mcps-core — consistent with P-TRUST-NO-CORE-REVOCATION-TRANSPORT
act_now.detail: P-TRUST-NO-CORE-REVOCATION-TRANSPORT forbids Core from defining any OCSP / revocation transport. This module implements full RFC 6960 online OCSP, but it is in mcps-proxy (a transport/deployment crate), compiled only under the non-default online_ocsp feature, and is a per-connection mTLS-leaf admission control — not part of mcps-core's object-signature trust model. Placement is therefore compliant. Verified the trust-chain is fail-closed (Unknown denies under hard-fail; Revoked always denies even under soft_fail; signature/identity/CertID-binding/freshness/nonce all gate before a Good is honored; SSRF guard on attacker-influenced AIA URL). No conformance violation; recorded for traceability that the OCSP claim is a Section-4-surface proxy feature, not a Core revocation transport.

act_now.title: Persistent-inner fail_closed error body swallows serialization failure via unwrap_or_default, yielding an empty frame
act_now.detail: PersistentSubprocessInner::fail_closed builds a JSON-RPC internal-error object and serializes it with serde_json::to_vec(...).unwrap_or_default(). On the (practically unreachable) serialization failure this returns an empty Vec rather than a structured error frame. Downstream this is response bytes the proxy will sign/return; an empty body is not a JSON-RPC frame and could confuse the caller's response pipeline. Not a key/identity leak and the input is a fixed literal so to_vec cannot realistically fail, but a hardcoded constant fallback error frame would be strictly more fail-closed than an empty buffer.

act_now.title: check_scope reads method/tool/arguments from the raw untrusted request, but these fields are not bound into the object signature scope by this layer
act_now.detail: check_scope extracts request.method, params.name and params.arguments directly from the original JSON-RPC `request` Value and matches them against the granted operations. The policy layer assumes Core already object-signature-verified that request (P-VERIFY-EVERY-MESSAGE / P-SIGN-WHOLE-OBJECT cover method+params because the whole object is signed). This is correct ONLY if the `request` passed to evaluate() is the canonical signed object, not an attacker-mutable copy. The contract is documented (evaluator doc: 'ORIGINAL JSON-RPC request object') but nothing in mcps-policy enforces that the request matches verified.request_hash. A caller that passes a tampered request body (different tool/args than what was signed) alongside a genuine VerifiedRequest would have scope evaluated against the tampered values. Flagging as info because binding the request to the signature is Core/sidecar's job, but the trust assumption is implicit and unchecked at this boundary.

act_now.title: Authorization binding order does not match the normative P-AUTHZ-BINDINGS sequence (revocation checked before scope, signature checked first)
act_now.detail: P-AUTHZ-BINDINGS specifies the evaluate order as grantee==signer, subject==on_behalf_of, audience==verified audience, validity window, revocation, then scope. evaluate_inner runs: issuer-signature verify, then grantee/subject/audience, then window, then revocation, then scope. This is a superset (signature verify is an extra correct first step) and the relative order of the catalog-named checks matches, so this is not a security regression — every binding is enforced and it fails closed at the first failure. Noted only as a documentation/order-conformance observation; the extra signature-first step is sound (an unsigned/forged artifact must never have its claims trusted).

act_now.title: Transport binding of signer to peer identity is not performed in mcps-transport (client side has no signer); ensure server-side enforcement is not assumed here
act_now.detail: P-TRANSPORT-BIND-SIGNER requires binding the request signer to the verified mTLS peer identity when transport binding is enabled. mcps-transport is client-side and explicitly does no signing (signing stays in mcps-host), so the signer<->peer binding must be enforced on the SERVER/sidecar side (out of this unit's scope). This is correct separation, but worth flagging that nothing in this client transport carries or asserts the signer identity over the channel — the binding check is entirely the proxy's responsibility and must not be presumed delivered by this crate. P-TRANSPORT-NOT-REPLACE-SIG holds: this transport does only mTLS and never substitutes for object-signature/Phase-5 authorization.

defer.title: SystemNonceSource panics on CSPRNG failure (expect) in production library code
defer.detail: `getrandom::getrandom(out).expect("OS CSPRNG (getrandom) must be available to sign requests")` is reached on every production nonce draw via HostSession::next_nonce -> SystemNonceSource::fill. getrandom can genuinely fail (early boot before entropy pool init, seccomp sandbox blocking the getrandom syscall, exhausted fds for /dev/urandom fallback). On failure this panics rather than returning a Result. NonceSource::fill returns (), so the trait cannot surface an error and the only escape is panic — a design-level fail-stop. Per P-NO-PANIC-LIB non-test library code MUST NOT use expect()/panic! and must return Result via the error taxonomy. Failing closed (no predictable nonce) is correct intent, but a panic unwinding through the signing call is a reliability/DoS hazard and violates the no-panic rule; the trait should return Result so the session maps it to an McpsError.

defer.title: Production nonce source panics on CSPRNG failure instead of failing closed with an McpsError
defer.detail: SystemNonceSource::fill (the production NonceSource for HostSession) calls getrandom::getrandom(out).expect(...). This is non-test library code on the live signing path; P-NO-PANIC-LIB forbids expect()/panic! in non-test library code and requires returning a Result from the frozen taxonomy. The NonceSource::fill trait signature returns (), so there is no fail-closed channel at all: an OS-CSPRNG outage aborts the process via panic rather than surfacing a recoverable McpsError. The session's next_nonce()->sign_request()->session.sign_request() chain can therefore panic on entropy unavailability. The comment acknowledges 'fail loudly rather than degrade silently' but the conformant behavior is a structured fail-closed error, not a panic. Recommend NonceSource::fill return Result<(), McpsError> and map getrandom errors to a fail-closed taxonomy error.

defer.title: SystemNonceSource::fill panics via expect() on CSPRNG failure in non-test library code
defer.detail: `getrandom::getrandom(out).expect("OS CSPRNG (getrandom) must be available to sign requests")` is a panic in non-test library code, violating P-NO-PANIC-LIB (core-spec §14: non-test library code MUST NOT use unwrap/expect/panic and MUST return Result). The NonceSource::fill trait signature returns () and cannot surface a Result, so the panic propagates up through HostSession::next_nonce -> sign_request, which DO return Result<_, McpsError>. A host process whose OS RNG is transiently unavailable (early boot, seccomp-restricted sandbox, exhausted fd for /dev/urandom fallback paths on some platforms) aborts the signing thread rather than returning a clean error. Failing loudly (never emit a predictable nonce) is the right SECURITY default, but the mechanism should be a typed McpsError up the signing path, not a panic — an unwind in a server task / across an FFI or transport boundary is a DoS surface. Recommend making NonceSource::fill fallible (Result<(), McpsError>) so signing fails closed with a typed error.

defer.title: SSRF guard misses non-dotted-decimal IP encodings (octal/hex/integer) for cert-AIA OCSP URLs
defer.detail: host_is_public() only treats a host as private when host.parse::<IpAddr>() succeeds AND matches a private range; otherwise (Err) it returns true ('a real hostname'). But std IpAddr::parse rejects the legacy IPv4 encodings that the OS resolver / ureq's getaddrinfo will still interpret as loopback/metadata: integer form http://2130706433/ (=127.0.0.1), hex http://0x7f000001/, octal http://0177.0.0.1/, and short forms like http://127.1/. All of these make host_is_public return true and pass aia_responder_url_is_safe(), so the #4078 (M14) SSRF guard is bypassed for an attacker-controlled leaf-cert AIA URL — the blocking serve thread is pointed at 127.0.0.1 / 169.254.169.254 / 10.0.0.0/8 etc. The guard also does not reject 100.64.0.0/10 CGNAT (is_shared). Blast radius is limited (a Good still requires the full RFC 6960 §3.2 signature trust chain to admit), but the explicit purpose of M14 was to stop the blocking thread reaching internal hosts at all. Canonicalize the host to an IpAddr before the public-range check, or reject hosts that are all-numeric / hex / octal.

defer.title: Replay TTL round-up / non-positive-remaining pre-rejection (P-V03-REPLAY-TTL-ROUNDUP) not enforced in the pipeline
defer.detail: Step 12 parses expires_at (line 202) and passes expires_at_unix straight into replay.check_and_insert. P-V03-REPLAY-TTL-ROUNDUP requires that a non-positive remaining TTL (already-stale) be rejected BEFORE the shared store is consulted, and that backend TTLs round UP. The pipeline performs freshness (step 9) earlier so a stale request is normally already rejected as ExpiredRequest before reaching step 12; however the pipeline delegates TTL computation/round-up entirely to the ReplayCache impl, passing only the absolute expires_at. For the in-core path this is acceptable (freshness gates staleness), but Core provides no remaining-TTL guard at the insert boundary, so the round-up/non-positive invariant is enforced (if at all) only in the backend adapter outside this unit. Flagged low as a coverage gap for the v0.3 shared-store tier.

defer.title: Parser materializes entire input into Vec<char> — O(n) memory amplification on untrusted input
defer.detail: Parser::new does `text.chars().collect()` into a `Vec<char>`, allocating 4 bytes per Unicode scalar for the WHOLE message up front (a ~4x blow-up over the input byte length for ASCII-heavy JSON-RPC). There is no input-size bound in canonical.rs; the only guard is MAX_PARSE_DEPTH (nesting), not total length. A large but shallow protected message (e.g. a multi-megabyte string value or array) forces a multi-megabyte char vector per verify call before any structural check runs. Since canonicalize() is invoked on raw_bytes at pipeline step 3 on every request/response, this is an attacker-influenced allocation. Not a panic, but a reliability/DoS amplifier; an upstream length cap should exist before canonicalize is reached.

defer.title: Two independent parsers (in-house raw + serde_json) must agree on the accepted value domain; divergence is unverified
defer.detail: The verify pipeline parses each message twice: once with serde_json (pipeline.rs:155, used to build the signed preimage) and once with the in-house raw parser (canonicalize(raw_bytes), step 3). Security depends on these two parsers accepting EXACTLY the same value domain and producing the same scalar values. The two implementations differ in subtle ways (e.g. the raw parser's number handling vs serde_json's i64/u64/f64 modeling at lines 137-152; surrogate/escape handling at 496-519 vs serde's). If any input is accepted by serde and produces a value tree that differs from what the raw parser would accept/produce, the signed preimage (from serde) could differ from what the dup/domain check validated (raw). The tests pin several agreement cases (json_value_helper_matches_raw_path_for_valid_input) but agreement is asserted only on hand-picked vectors, not enforced structurally. The robust design is a single parse path feeding both the domain check and the preimage. Flagging as a latent correctness/consistency risk rather than a confirmed bug.

defer.title: No length/size bound on input before full char-vector materialization
defer.detail: Parser::new collects the entire input into a Vec<char> (chars().collect(), line 308), and parse_number does self.chars[start..self.pos].iter().collect::<String>() (line 559). There is no upper bound on total input length in this unit; a multi-gigabyte JSON-RPC body would allocate a Vec<char> (4 bytes/char) plus intermediate String allocations, a memory-amplification DoS (~4x the byte length, before parsing even validates). Depth is bounded but breadth/total-size is not. This is acceptable IF an upstream transport/pipeline layer enforces a max message size before calling canonicalize; that bound was not verified in this unit and is not visible here. Flagging at low: canonicalization is the first untrusted-input touch point and a defense-in-depth size cap (or streaming) at this boundary would be prudent rather than relying solely on an upstream limit.

defer.title: json_rpc_error_object silent fallback discards the real id and error token
defer.detail: On the (practically unreachable) serde_json::to_vec failure path, the function returns a hardcoded byte literal with id:null and JSON-RPC code -32603, dropping both the caller-supplied request id and the frozen mcps.* wire token. A consumer would then see an internal-error code instead of the precise mcps verdict and lose request correlation. Not exploitable (a plain json! object always serializes), but the fallback degrades the error contract rather than preserving the verdict; consider building the fallback from `code` and `id` instead of a fixed literal.

defer.title: Replay cache retain-until is not bounded against non-positive / pre-stale expires_at
defer.detail: InMemoryReplayCache::check_and_insert computes retain_until = expires_at_unix.saturating_add(max_clock_skew_secs) and inserts unconditionally, with no check that the request is still within validity. P-V03-REPLAY-TTL-ROUNDUP requires that a non-positive remaining TTL (already-stale request) be rejected BEFORE the shared store is consulted. In the core pipeline this is mitigated because check_freshness (step 9) runs before the replay insert (step 12), so an already-expired request never reaches check_and_insert. However the cache itself enforces no floor: a caller invoking it directly (or a backend adapter modeled on it) could insert an entry with a retain_until already in the past, which prune() would immediately evict, defeating replay retention. The trait contract should state (or the impl should enforce) the pre-stale rejection rather than leaving it solely to pipeline ordering. Core-only ceiling, so low.

defer.title: check_and_insert atomicity is contractual only; non-atomic backend could admit concurrent replays
defer.detail: The ReplayCache trait doc states check_and_insert must 'Atomically check whether (signer,audience,nonce) was already seen and record it if not.' The in-memory reference impl is atomic only because it holds &mut self (exclusive). For a real shared/distributed backend (the P-V03 replay tiers) the atomicity is unverifiable from this trait alone: a check-then-insert that is not a single atomic store operation permits two concurrent first-sightings of the same nonce to both observe Fresh. P-REPLAY-AFTER-SIG/P-REPLAY-DETECT presume atomic test-and-set. The trait MUST be implemented with an atomic primitive (e.g. SET NX) downstream; nothing in core enforces it. Low: correctness rests on an out-of-crate contract the catalog treats as load-bearing.

defer.title: Malformed timestamp folds into expired_request rather than a parse/canonicalization error
defer.detail: parse_rfc3339_utc maps every malformed timestamp (bad grammar, out-of-range fields, numeric offset, trailing junk) to McpsError::ExpiredRequest. This is fail-closed (admits nothing), and the catalog's P-FRESHNESS-WINDOW says stale/future-dated -> expired_request, but a structurally malformed timestamp is arguably a JCS/value-domain violation that P-CANON-FAILCLOSED-BEFORE-SIG would route to canonicalization_failed. Folding malformed-format into expired_request is safe but conflates a syntax error with a freshness verdict in the error taxonomy. Verify the spec intends issued_at/expires_at format validation to occur in the JCS-domain step (canonicalization_failed) before freshness, otherwise the observed mcps.* token for a malformed timestamp will be expired_request, which may mislead conformance vectors.

defer.title: JSON-RPC error fallback drops request id and emits generic -32603 instead of frozen mcps token
defer.detail: json_rpc_error_object's serde serialization failure fallback returns a hardcoded {"id":null,"error":{"code":-32603}} literal. This loses the bound request id (correlation) and emits a JSON-RPC internal-error code with NO mcps_error token, diverging from the frozen MCP-S error envelope shape every server is supposed to emit identically. In practice the primary json!{} object always serializes so this branch is effectively dead, but if ever reached it produces a non-conformant error object. Low severity / borderline; flagged because the comment claims 'every server emits an identical error envelope' yet the fallback is not that envelope. The echoed details field reuses the wire code (a non-sensitive mcps.* token), so no secret/diagnostic leakage.

defer.title: id_key collapses serialization failures to empty string, risking pending-map key collision
defer.detail: `fn id_key(id: &Value) -> String { serde_json::to_string(id).unwrap_or_default() }` silently maps any serialization failure to "". serde_json::to_string of a Value effectively never fails, so this is latent rather than live, but if it ever did, two distinct ids would both key to "" — causing sign_request's duplicate-id guard (line 105) to spuriously reject a fresh id as ReplayDetected, and verify_response (line 171) to bind a response to the wrong pending hash. The fallback should be a structurally-unique encoding or an explicit error rather than a value-collapsing default.

defer.title: expires_at computed by unchecked i64 addition (overflow / no lifetime validation)
defer.detail: `let expires_unix = issued_unix + self.request_lifetime_secs;` is an unchecked i64 add. With a pathological clock value near i64::MAX or a very large request_lifetime_secs this overflows: release builds wrap (producing an expires_at far in the past, so every request is born already-expired and fails verifier freshness — fail-closed but silently broken); debug builds panic. Separately, HostSession::new accepts request_lifetime_secs with no validation, so a negative lifetime yields expires_unix < issued_unix (instantly-stale requests) with no error. Use checked_add and reject non-positive lifetimes at construction.

defer.title: Signature value is grafted via panic-prone serde_json Index assignment rather than checked insertion
defer.detail: sign_request grafts the signature with request["params"]["_meta"][REQUEST_META_KEY]["signature"]["value"] = Value::String(signature). serde_json's IndexMut panics if any intermediate path element is absent or is not an object. Here the full path is constructed in the json! literal a few lines above so it is present in practice, making this not currently reachable from external input — hence low. But it is non-test library code relying on a panic-on-missing-path operator on the signing hot path; a future change to envelope construction (or params shape) could turn it into an input-triggerable panic. A checked get_mut / insert would be conformant with P-NO-PANIC-LIB without relying on the literal staying in sync.

defer.title: id_key uses serde_json::to_string(id).unwrap_or_default(), silently collapsing un-serializable ids to the same empty key
defer.detail: `fn id_key(id: &Value) -> String { serde_json::to_string(id).unwrap_or_default() }` maps any id whose serialization fails to the empty string "". serde_json::to_string on a Value is effectively infallible for normal JSON, so this is benign in practice, but `unwrap_or_default()` means that IF two distinct ids ever produced a serialization error they collide on the empty key — causing a second sign_request to be wrongly rejected as ReplayDetected, or a response to bind to the wrong stored request_hash. The host also does not constrain `id` to the MCP-S id domain (string or safe integer, MCPS_SPEC §4) before keying/signing; an array/object/float id is accepted and keyed by its JSON text. Lower severity because the verifier-side JCS domain check rejects such requests downstream and the serialization error path is unreachable for valid Values. The host as signer should arguably reject a non-conformant id at sign time.

defer.title: Short HTTP body (EOF before Content-Length) is silently truncated and forwarded instead of erroring
defer.detail: read_http_request loops reading the body until body.len() >= content_length, but on a premature EOF (stream.read returns n==0) it breaks (line 674-676) and then calls body.truncate(content_length) (line 679). When the body is SHORTER than content_length, truncate is a no-op and the under-length body is returned to the handler as if complete. This is fail-closed for security (incomplete JSON => verify_request fails => inner never reached), but it conflates 'client sent a short body' with 'valid request', producing a confusing canonicalization/parse error rather than a clean transport-level short-read error. Compare the over-length path which explicitly errors. Prefer returning an UnexpectedEof error when body.len() < content_length at EOF.

defer.title: build_forwarded_request None-arm uses Value index-assignment that is a no-op when params is not an object
defer.detail: In the defensive None arm of build_forwarded_request, when request["params"]["_meta"].as_object_mut() returns None the code does request["params"]["_meta"] = json!({ VERIFIED_META_KEY: context_value }). serde_json's IndexMut on a Value only inserts when the parent is an Object; if params is present but a NON-object scalar (e.g. a verified request whose params was a string/number), indexing params["_meta"] does not create the path and the assignment is dropped, so NO verified-context block is injected and the inner server receives the request without the proxy-asserted identity. A verified MCP-S tools/call always has an object params so this is unreachable in practice, but the comment claims the arm 'synthesizes a _meta carrying only the fresh context' — which is not guaranteed for a non-object params. The sole-writer guarantee (strip caller verified + inject fresh) would silently not inject here. Make the arm explicitly coerce params to an object (or return CanonicalizationFailed) so the injection cannot be silently dropped.

defer.title: Outgoing response id is sourced from raw inbound bytes, not the verified envelope
defer.detail: build_signed_response sets response["id"] = id_value, where id_value was parsed from the ORIGINAL inbound bytes in handle_with_transport (proxy.rs:211-214), independent of what verify_request canonically accepted. The inner-error doc-comment and test claim the id 'correlates to the verified request', but VerifiedRequest carries no id field, so the proxy cannot cross-check the parsed top-level id against the signed object. The real integrity tie is request_hash binding (P-RESPONSE-HASH-BIND), which makes a wrong id non-exploitable; flagging low because the stated 'verified-request id correlation' invariant is actually 'inbound-bytes id correlation' and is unverifiable here.

defer.title: Outgoing response id is echoed from the raw inbound parse rather than from the verified request
defer.detail: id_value is parsed from the raw inbound bytes via serde_json::from_slice (211-214, last-wins on duplicate keys) and is the id placed on the signed response (378) and on every error object. VerifiedRequest exposes no id field, so the proxy cannot bind the outgoing id to the canonical verified id directly; it relies on request_hash (which covers the canonical id) for tamper-evidence. Because duplicate-key inputs are already rejected by verify_request step 3 before this value is trusted for dispatch, the last-wins re-parse here is not exploitable on the success path. On the FAILURE path (verify_result is Err) the error object's id comes from this unverified, possibly duplicate-keyed parse — but an error response carries no security-relevant authority, so impact is cosmetic. Flagged low for the verified/raw provenance gap.

defer.title: RequestHeaders::parse silently drops HTTP obs-fold continuation lines and bare-CR-joined content, weakening duplicate/control-char defenses
defer.detail: parse() tokenizes the header block with str::lines(), which splits only on \n (stripping a trailing \r). It is on the live serve path (tls.rs:524, 591). Two correctness gaps: (1) An HTTP/1.1 obs-fold continuation line (a header value continued on a following line starting with SP/TAB) is treated as an independent line; if it lacks a ':' it is silently ignored (losing part of a value), and if it happens to contain ':' it is parsed as a spurious separate header. Obs-fold is deprecated and should be rejected, not silently swallowed. (2) A bare CR (\r not followed by \n) does not split a logical line, so two wire fields joined by a lone CR collapse into one parse line; only the part before the first ':' becomes the name. Because the duplicate-trust-header defense in ReverseProxyMtlsProvider relies on count()==1 over the parsed list, any tokenization divergence between this ad-hoc parser and a real HTTP framer could let a smuggled second copy of the trust header be hidden from count(). The parser does not fail closed on malformed structure (it ignores bad lines); it should reject a header block it cannot cleanly tokenize rather than parse a prefix of it.

defer.title: split_xfcc_pairs treats an unbalanced double quote as an open quoted span for the entire remainder, disabling pair separation
defer.detail: split_xfcc_pairs toggles in_quotes on every '"' with no validation that quotes are balanced. An XFCC value with an odd number of quotes (e.g. a Subject="... with no closing quote) leaves in_quotes=true for the rest of the string, so all subsequent ',' and ';' stop acting as pair delimiters and the tail collapses into one pair. This is generally fail-closed (a malformed blob is unlikely to extract a clean URI/DNS/CN), but it is unvalidated parsing of operator-trusted-upstream data: an unbalanced-quote header should be rejected as malformed rather than reinterpreted. No test covers an unbalanced-quote XFCC value.

defer.title: unescape_rfc2253_value drops the backslash on \<hexpair> byte escapes instead of decoding them, risking direct-vs-XFCC CN divergence
defer.detail: unescape_rfc2253_value resolves \<char> by emitting the next char verbatim and is documented to pass \<hexpair> escapes through 'best-effort by dropping the backslash'. RFC2253 \HH is a hex-encoded byte; a real DN library (the x509-parser direct-TLS path reads the decoded CN attribute, tls.rs:312) yields the decoded byte/char, whereas this code yields the two hex digits with the backslash removed (e.g. \41 -> '41' not 'A'). For a CN that legitimately contains a hex-escaped character, the XFCC strategy and the direct-TLS strategy resolve the SAME certificate to DIFFERENT identity strings, breaking the cross-strategy parity the module relies on for the binding policy to admit one configured signer. Uncommon in CN values but a real correctness divergence under the stated M23 parity requirement.

defer.title: XFCC conflict detection covers only the selected field; non-selected duplicate/garbage pairs are silently ignored
defer.detail: `split_xfcc_pairs` (line 463) flattens ALL cert elements and pairs into one stream, and `parse_xfcc` only inspects pairs whose key is in `wanted` (line 404). Conflicts are detected solely across occurrences of the selected field (line 429). A header that carries two different cert elements where only ONE has the selected field (e.g. element A has `URI=spiffe://good`, element B has only `DNS=...`) is accepted as `good` even though the two elements describe different peers — element boundaries are deliberately discarded.
