ADR-MCPS-023 treats asserted-identity as attacker-controlled-unless-trusted; while the upstream is operator-asserted-trusted, flattening loses the per-element binding that would let a multi-element XFCC be checked for internal consistency. Borderline: relies entirely on the trusted-upstream assumption to be safe; flag at low.

defer.title: XFCC parser flattens all cert elements, so a non-leaf element can supply the identity when the leaf lacks the selected field
defer.detail: parse_xfcc (and split_xfcc_pairs at line 463) intentionally flatten ALL cert elements and ALL pairs into one stream, extracting the configured field from every element and accepting a single consistent value. XFCC can legitimately carry multiple elements (the client leaf plus intermediate-proxy certs that each may include By=/URI=). The TransportIdentity doc (lines 55-56) states identity is the LEAF of the chain, but the flattening logic does not pin extraction to the first/leaf element — if the leaf element omits URI= but a later element (e.g. an intermediate ingress identity) carries URI=, that later element's value becomes the bound identity. With a single trusted upstream emitting one leaf this is benign, but it widens the trusted parse surface beyond the leaf the property describes. Conflict across elements fails closed (line 429), which mitigates the multi-distinct-value case but not the leaf-absent / single-non-leaf-value case.

defer.title: RFC2253 hex-byte escape (\\XX) is silently coerced rather than decoded, weakening CN parity claim
defer.detail: unescape_rfc2253_value handles \<char> by dropping the backslash and keeping the next char, but RFC2253 also allows \<hexpair> byte escapes (e.g. CN=\41 for 'A'). The doc (lines 553-555) admits these are passed through best-effort by dropping the backslash, so CN=\41 yields '41' not 'A'. The stated parity requirement is that this XFCC CN equals the direct-TLS extract_identity CN for the SAME cert. If the direct-TLS x509 parser decodes hex escapes to the real byte while this path yields the literal hex digits, the SAME certificate resolves to two different identity strings across strategies — the exact identity-confusion failure M23/#4080 was meant to close. Confined to CnLegacy (deprecated) with hex-escaped CNs, hence low, but it is a real cross-strategy identity-confusion gap, not merely cosmetic.

defer.title: compute_ttl_ms clamps an already-stale retain-until to 1ms and proceeds instead of the store rejecting before consultation
defer.detail: P-V03-REPLAY-TTL-ROUNDUP states: 'a non-positive remaining TTL means the request is already stale and MUST be rejected before the shared store is consulted.' compute_ttl_ms (line 78-79) instead computes ttl_secs = max(0, retain_until - now) and clamps to a minimal 1ms (.max(1)), so insert_if_absent still records the nonce in Redis for an already-stale request rather than rejecting it. The code relies on the upstream freshness step (P-FRESHNESS-WINDOW) to have already rejected stale requests, so by contract a non-positive TTL should never reach here; but if it does, this store admits and stores it (with a 1ms key) rather than failing closed at the store boundary the property describes. The 1ms-clamp rationale ('answer a same-instant racing replay') is defensible operationally but is not the property's mandated behavior. Reliability/spec-alignment note, not an exploitable bug given the upstream freshness gate.

defer.title: SharedReplayCache with InMemoryAtomicReplayStore has no TTL and no wired prune — unbounded keyspace growth
defer.detail: InMemoryAtomicReplayStore::insert_if_absent (line 233-250) ignores now_unix and never auto-expires; eviction is only via the explicit prune() (line 215). SharedReplayCache (the ReplayCache wrapper) never calls prune and exposes no prune hook on the trait, so when the in-memory reference store backs SharedReplayCache in a single-process deployment, entries accumulate indefinitely unless some external owner holds the concrete store and prunes it on a schedule. Unlike the Redis backend (server-side PX TTL) and Durable/InMemoryReplayCache (documented explicit prune), there is no documented caller obligation surfaced through the SharedReplayCache type. Memory-growth/DoS hazard if the in-memory shared store is used without an out-of-band pruner. The module docs note this store is the only in-tree backend and not multi-node-capable, but do not flag the missing prune wiring.

defer.title: WAIT-quorum failure leaves the nonce written on the primary, so a legitimate retry of the same request is rejected as replay (no over-claim, but a fail-closed availability/correctness gap)
defer.detail: In insert_if_absent, when SET NX PX succeeds (Fresh on primary) but WAIT returns insufficient acks, the code returns OpAttempt::Fatal(Unavailable) WITHOUT deleting the just-written key. This correctly fails closed for the current request (P-V03-REPLAY-OPERR-FAILCLOSED satisfied). However the nonce now exists on the primary with its full PX TTL. A client that legitimately retries the identical signed request (same nonce) will hit the existing key and get Ok(None) -> ReplayDecision::Replay -> mcps.replay_detected, not replay_cache_unavailable. The comment at lines 416-420 acknowledges the SET+WAIT op is non-idempotent under retry and deliberately does NOT delete, but the consequence is that a transient under-replication turns a retryable Unavailable into a permanent (for the freshness window) replay rejection of a never-accepted request. Conformance-wise the strong property (never Fresh on insufficient acks) holds; the gap is that the failed insert is not rolled back, unlike DurableReplayCache::check_and_insert which DOES roll back its in-memory insert on persist failure (durable_replay.rs:118-124). Consider a best-effort DEL of the key on WAIT failure before failing closed.

defer.title: Shared-store insert does not reject already-stale (non-positive remaining TTL) requests; records them as Fresh with a 1ms TTL
defer.detail: compute_ttl_ms() clamps a non-positive remaining TTL to a minimal 1ms (`.max(1)`) and insert_if_absent then proceeds to SET the key and return ReplayDecision::Fresh. P-V03-REPLAY-TTL-ROUNDUP requires that a non-positive remaining TTL means the request is ALREADY STALE and MUST be rejected before the shared store is consulted. The store relies entirely on an upstream freshness check (P-FRESHNESS-WINDOW) to have already rejected such a request, but provides no defense-in-depth: if the freshness gate is ever bypassed or mis-ordered, an expired nonce is admitted as Fresh rather than failing closed. The in-memory store (shared_replay.rs InMemoryAtomicReplayStore) has the same property. This is a borderline defensive gap, not an active fail-open, because freshness is enforced earlier in the canonical pipeline.

defer.title: Trust-cache key composition uses a non-injective '#' separator (signer/key_id boundary can be forged)
defer.detail: BoundedTrustCache::compose_key formats the cache key as `format!("{signer}#{key_id}")`. This concatenation is not injective: signer="a#b", key_id="c" and signer="a", key_id="b#c" both produce "a#b#c". A cache hit on the colliding key would return a cached Active VerificationKey resolved for a DIFFERENT (signer,key_id) pair. The same non-injective keying exists in mcps-core/src/resolver.rs:144, so within a consistent deployment the cache returns the same answer the backend would; the divergence risk is bounded by that shared (also non-injective) keying. Contrast shared_replay.rs::composite_key, which deliberately length-prefixes each field ("<len>:<field>") to guarantee injectivity precisely against this aliasing. The trust path should adopt the same length-prefixed (or hashed length-prefixed) keying so an attacker who controls signer/key_id text cannot engineer a cache-key collision that serves a key bound to a different identity. Identity binding is security-decisional here (response-signer admission), so the weaker keying is worth fixing.

defer.title: BoundedTrustCache caches positive Active resolutions for the full window T even when the inner resolver applies a time-bounded validity window
defer.detail: When BoundedTrustCache wraps a KeySetTrustResolver, an Active resolution is cached for t_secs (up to RECOMMENDED_MAX_T_SECS = 300). The inner KeySetTrustResolver.resolve (authorized_keyset.rs:356-364) enforces the per-entry validity window (valid_from/valid_until) against its own clock at resolve time, but once the cache has stored Active(key), subsequent hits within T bypass the inner resolver entirely (cached() returns before line 239). Therefore an entry whose valid_until elapses DURING the cached window keeps resolving as Active for up to T-? seconds past its expiry. ADR-MCPS-021 explicitly bounds revocation exposure to T, so this is within the documented window and not a fail-open per se, but the key-validity-window expiry is silently widened by up to T. Operators reasoning about valid_until as a hard cutoff should know the effective cutoff is valid_until + (residual cache window). Flagged as low because it is bounded by the same T the ADR sanctions.

defer.title: AmortizedSession caches a session known to have just failed on the retry's SessionInvalid path
defer.detail: In with_session, after a transient SessionInvalid the code opens a fresh session and runs op once more. Line 179 unconditionally executes `*guard = Some(session);` BEFORE matching the retry result. If the retry ALSO returns SessionInvalid (the just-opened session immediately went bad, e.g. device removed), the error is surfaced (correct, fail-closed) but the now-believed-dead session is still left cached. The next operation's first attempt will reuse that dead session, get SessionInvalid, and self-heal via one more re-open — so it is self-correcting and not a fail-open, but it caches a handle that is known to have failed and wastes one extra round-trip. Consider clearing the cache (`*guard = None`) when the retry returns SessionInvalid.

defer.title: EnvKeySource::read uses std::env::remove_var which is racy/unsound under concurrency
defer.detail: EnvKeySource::read calls std::env::remove_var(var) immediately after reading the secret (defense-in-depth to shrink the inheritance window). std::env::{set_var,remove_var} are documented as not thread-safe (and are unsafe as of the 2024 edition) — concurrent getenv/setenv in another thread is UB. EnvKeySource is gated behind the non-default dev_env_key_source feature and is dev/CI-only, and the read normally happens at single-threaded startup, so real-world exposure is low; but if any other thread (e.g. a logging/telemetry init) touches the environment concurrently this is undefined behavior, not merely a data race on a value. Acceptable given the dev-only gating but worth noting.

defer.title: Pkcs11KeySource caches a fresh session even when the retried op returns a fatal/invalid error
defer.detail: In AmortizedSession::with_session, after a SessionInvalid first attempt the code opens a fresh session, runs the retry op, then unconditionally stores that session back into the cache (`*guard = Some(session);`) BEFORE matching on the retry result. If the retry itself returns SessionOpError::SessionInvalid (a second transient fault), the error is surfaced (correct, fail-closed) but a session that the op just reported as invalid is left cached. The next call's first attempt will run op() on this already-suspected-invalid handle, get SessionInvalid again, and re-open — so it self-heals on the following call and is never treated as Fresh. No security bypass (fail-closed is preserved; no fabricated signature), but it is a minor correctness wart: a known-bad session is retained one extra cycle. Worth tightening to drop the cache on a retry SessionInvalid.

defer.title: close_fds_above_stdio clamps to RLIMIT_NOFILE soft limit, which can skip high-numbered inherited fds if the limit was lowered
defer.detail: The pre-exec fd-closing hook (M15) computes max_fd from the current RLIMIT_NOFILE soft limit (clamped to HARD_CAP_FD 65536, fallback 1024). The soft limit is the count of allowable open fds, not the highest fd number that can exist: an fd opened earlier while the limit was higher, then the soft limit lowered, could have a numeric value >= rlim_cur and would NOT be closed by the `fd <= max_fd` loop, surviving exec into the inner. In practice the proxy does not lower its own NOFILE, and apply_rlimits sets the CHILD's limits via a separate pre_exec hook (registered before this one) which would itself reduce rlim_cur seen here — but ordering means the rlimit hook runs first, so this hook could read the already-lowered child limit and under-close. Defense-in-depth gap rather than a guaranteed leak; the documented non-containment boundary (P-INNER-NONCONTAINMENT) already disclaims full containment. Consider iterating to the hard cap or using close_range/proc to be exhaustive.

defer.title: close-fds pre-exec hook clamps the close range to 65536, leaking inherited fds above that cap into the inner server
defer.detail: close_fds_above_stdio() computes max_fd as min(RLIMIT_NOFILE soft limit, HARD_CAP_FD=65536) and closes fd 3..=max_fd. The stated purpose (M15/#4080) is to guarantee the inner inherits ONLY stdio so a connected proxy socket the seccomp egress filter cannot revoke is never leaked across exec. If the process soft RLIMIT_NOFILE exceeds 65536 (common on servers, where it is set to 1<<20) and any descriptor with a value above 65536 is open and lacks O_CLOEXEC, it survives exec into the inner — exactly the leak this hook exists to prevent. The clamp trades the security guarantee for a bounded loop; on a high-fd-limit host the 'authoritative close' is not authoritative. A close_range(2)/closefrom-style mechanism, or honoring the true soft limit, would close the gap without an unbounded loop.

defer.title: EnvKeySource uses process-global std::env::remove_var which is unsound under concurrent env access
defer.detail: EnvKeySource::read() calls std::env::remove_var(var) immediately after reading the seed/PEM material (defense in depth). std::env::set_var/remove_var mutate a process-global table not synchronized against concurrent std::env::var reads from other threads; current Rust editions are marking these unsafe precisely because such concurrent access is UB. The proxy is multi-threaded (stderr-drain threads, serve loop). This is gated behind the non-default dev_env_key_source feature and documented as dev/CI-only, which bounds blast radius, but the remove_var-during-startup pattern is a latent data race if any thread touches the environment concurrently. Reading all env material before spawning any thread avoids it.

defer.title: Default inner working directory is the shared system temp dir, a world-writable starting point
defer.detail: default_working_dir() returns std::env::temp_dir() (typically /tmp), used whenever --inner-working-dir is unset. This correctly avoids leaking the proxy's own cwd (the stated goal), but /tmp is world-writable and shared with every other local user/process: an inner server that resolves relative paths, drops temp files, or trusts files in cwd is launched into a directory a local attacker can pre-populate or race (predictable-filename / symlink attacks). The module honestly disclaims this is 'a controlled starting point, not a sandbox,' so it is not a containment-claim violation, but a per-launch private subdirectory (mkdtemp) would be a strictly safer default than the shared temp root.

defer.title: extract_body returns the entire response (headers included) when the HTTP header terminator is absent
defer.detail: extract_body searches for b"\r\n\r\n" and, when not found, falls back to `unwrap_or(0)`, returning the whole buffer as the body. A truncated or malformed HTTP/1.1 response (e.g. headers received but the terminator never arrives before EOF) is handed to the caller as a 'body' that is actually raw HTTP headers. The signing/verification layer downstream will reject it, but the transport silently mislabels framing failure as a body rather than surfacing a framing error. The function also ignores Content-Length and does not validate the status line, so a non-200 error response body is returned indistinguishably from a success body.

defer.title: expires_at parsed twice; second parse error after a valid signature is a latent token-ordering risk
defer.detail: expires_at is parsed in check_freshness (step 9, time.rs:184) and AGAIN at step 12 (pipeline.rs:202) via parse_rfc3339_utc before the replay insert. Because step 9 already succeeded with the same string and same parser, the step-12 parse cannot realistically fail, so this is currently harmless. But it is a redundant re-parse of an already-validated value, and if the two call sites ever diverge (different parser/normalization) a step-12 parse failure would surface as its own error AFTER signature verification succeeded yet BEFORE the replay insert, which is acceptable (no nonce burned) but wastes the verified-signature work. Prefer threading the already-parsed expires epoch from step 9 into step 12.

defer.title: request_hash recomputed AFTER replay insert succeeds
defer.detail: On the success path, computed_request_hash = request_hash(&value) is computed at line 215, i.e. AFTER replay.check_and_insert has already inserted the nonce (line 203-212). request_hash internally rebuilds the request signing preimage; that preimage already succeeded at step 11 (line 193), so this call cannot fail in practice. If it ever could (e.g. a future refactor makes preimage building fallible on a second pass), the '?' on line 215 would error out a request whose nonce was already burned, turning a transient build error into a permanent replay rejection on retry. Compute request_hash from the already-built step-11 preimage instead of re-deriving it after the replay insert.

defer.title: reject_notification treats any non-object top-level value as a notification, masking batch/garbage as NotificationForbidden if reached out of order
defer.detail: reject_notification (constraints.rs:68-73) uses msg.get("id") which returns None for any non-object (array, string, number, bool, null) -> NotificationForbidden. In the canonical pipeline reject_batch runs first (step 1) so an array is caught as BatchForbidden, and this is documented as intentional defensive fallthrough. Correct as currently ordered; noting only that the function's verdict for a bare scalar/array is NotificationForbidden, which is a fail-closed but semantically loose token if this function were ever invoked before reject_batch. No action needed while step order holds.

defer.title: serde_json::from_slice parse failure maps to CanonicalizationFailed before the dedicated canonicalize() raw-bytes check runs
defer.detail: Both verify_request (line 154) and verify_response (line 241) parse with serde_json::from_slice and map any parse error to McpsError::CanonicalizationFailed. This occurs BEFORE step 1/step 3. serde_json by default is last-wins on duplicate keys and does not reject them, so the duplicate-key rejection relied on by P-CANON-DUPKEY-REJECT must come entirely from the later canonicalize(raw_bytes) call (line 164/245) operating on raw bytes, not from this serde parse. This is correct in principle (the raw-bytes canonicalize is the authoritative dup-key path), but note the value model used for envelope extraction and preimage building is the last-wins serde Value; correctness depends on canonicalize() having already rejected any duplicate-key input before the serde Value is trusted. Ordering is right (canonicalize at step 3 precedes extract at step 4), so no violation; recorded for traceability of P-CANON-DUPKEY-REJECT enforcement living outside this unit.

defer.title: Fail-closed step order in verify_request matches the normative P-FAILCLOSED-ORDER sequence
defer.detail: Positive conformance note: verify_request enforces the exact normative order — reject_batch (157), reject_notification (161), canonicalize/JCS-domain on raw bytes (164), extract_request_envelope = locate+deny-unknown+version (167), required-field checks (170-172), audience (175), freshness (180), trust resolve (188), preimage+Ed25519 verify (199), replay check_and_insert LAST (203). Signature verification (199) precedes replay insert (203), satisfying P-REPLAY-AFTER-SIG so invalid-signature traffic cannot burn nonces; the test bad_signature_precedes_replay_and_does_not_burn_nonce (line 619) exercises this. No reordering or short-circuit that skips a step was found.

defer.title: Fail-closed step order, error mappings, and no-panic discipline are correct across both pipelines
defer.detail: Confirmed (not a defect): verify_request executes the exact normative order from P-FAILCLOSED-ORDER — batch reject (158), notification reject (161), JCS-domain incl dup-key on raw bytes (164), locate/unknown-field/version (167), required-field presence/format (170-172), audience (175), freshness (180), trust resolve (188), Ed25519 verify over signature.value-stripped preimage (193-199), then replay check_and_insert LAST (203). The replay insert is strictly after a successful signature verification, so invalid-signature traffic cannot burn nonces (verified by test bad_signature_precedes_replay_and_does_not_burn_nonce). Resolver errors map fail-closed (NotFound/Revoked/MalformedKey->ActorBindingFailed, Unavailable->TrustResolverUnavailable; never allow) and ReplayCacheError->ReplayCacheUnavailable, never Fresh. serde parse failure maps to CanonicalizationFailed (fail closed). No unwrap/expect/panic in the non-test library paths; timestamp parse failures fail closed to ExpiredRequest. verify_response order (canonicalize, locate/unknown-field, alg, resolve, verify, request_hash) matches P-FAILCLOSED-RESPONSE-ORDER. Replay key uses the verified envelope (signer,audience,nonce) after the audience match. This finding records the positive verification for traceability.

defer.title: from_serde_value relies on serde_json's default (non-arbitrary_precision) number model; an arbitrary_precision build would silently change number acceptance
defer.detail: from_serde_value_at depends on serde_json modeling numbers as i64/u64/f64 so that any non-integer falls into the final f64 branch and is rejected (line 149-151). If serde_json's `arbitrary_precision` feature were ever enabled (directly or via a transitive dependency's feature unification in the Cargo workspace), Number::as_i64/as_u64 semantics change and a large/decimal number could be carried as a raw-precision string, defeating the integer-only rejection on the serde path. The raw path is unaffected, so the divergence would only manifest on the canonicalize_json_value/preimage path. Worth a build-time assertion or an explicit `default-features=false` pin and a comment; currently an implicit dependency on a feature flag.

defer.title: b64url_decode does not reject trailing-bit non-canonical encodings beyond what the engine enforces
defer.detail: b64url_decode maps any decode error to CanonicalizationFailed and relies on URL_SAFE_NO_PAD to reject padding and non-alphabet chars (tests confirm). The base64 crate's NO_PAD engine does reject overlong inputs and (in recent versions) non-zero trailing bits, but this is version-dependent behavior the code does not pin or test for (e.g. a final quantum with set padding bits). For hash-id parsing (hash.rs) the 32-byte length check at parse_hash_id provides a second gate, but for general signature-value decoding elsewhere the canonicalization of base64 (no malleability) rests on the crate version's trailing-bit policy. Recommend an explicit test pinning rejection of a non-canonical trailing-bit encoding to lock the contract. Informational; no confirmed defect.

defer.title: Object/array member separators accept whitespace-then-comma but the empty-container fast path reads peek before the loop's skip_ws — verify no value-after-comma-EOF mis-handling
defer.detail: In parse_object/parse_array the post-value separator handling uses `match self.bump()` for ',' / '}' / ']' (lines 425-428, 447-450). A ',' continues the loop, which then calls skip_ws() and parses the next value; a trailing comma before '}' is correctly rejected because after ',' the loop expects a '"' key (object) or a value (array), and '}'/']' there fails — tests trailing_comma_rejected confirm. Reviewed for an off-by-one early-return: the empty-object/array fast path (lines 405/438) correctly bumps the closing brace and returns. No defect found; recorded as reviewed to document that the separator state machine was checked for early-return / off-by-one errors and is sound.

defer.title: b64url_decode and parse_hash_id map structural decode failures to CanonicalizationFailed, which is correct for the JCS domain but conflates encoding-format errors with JCS-domain errors
defer.detail: b64url_decode maps any malformed Base64URL to McpsError::CanonicalizationFailed, and parse_hash_id (hash.rs:35) reuses it for a missing prefix / bad body / wrong digest length. The module docs (encoding.rs:7-12) correctly note that security-critical signature decoders must NOT reuse this mapping and instead map to InvalidSignature — and crypto does so. This is conformant: a malformed authorization_hash/request_hash is a structural JCS-domain failure, matching P-CANON-FAILCLOSED-BEFORE-SIG (canonicalization_failed, not invalid_signature). Flagged only as an observation that the single CanonicalizationFailed sink covers both base64 and hash-format malformation; verify callers depending on a distinct token (e.g. response_hash_mismatch vs canonicalization_failed) are not relying on parse_hash_id to surface a different error for a malformed-but-present hash.

defer.title: Number token i64 parse silently caps reachable range below documented safe-integer bound via two overlapping checks (defense-in-depth, not a bug)
defer.detail: parse_number collects the digit token and does token.parse::<i64>() then check_safe_integer(). A digit string longer than i64::MAX fails .parse() -> CanonicalizationFailed before the safe-integer check; values in (2^53-1, i64::MAX] pass .parse() then correctly fail check_safe_integer(). Net behavior is correct (everything > 2^53-1 rejected), but two layered rejection mechanisms mean the safe-integer error is only reached for a sub-band. Verified the serde path (from_serde_value_at, lines 141-152) applies the same bound for i64/u64 and rejects f64 outright, so raw and serde paths agree on the accepted integer domain. No defect; noting for auditor traceability of the range invariant.

defer.title: b64url_decode maps malformed input to CanonicalizationFailed; correct only because security-critical callers override the mapping
defer.detail: b64url_decode returns McpsError::CanonicalizationFailed for any decode failure. If a security-critical caller (e.g. signature.value or key bytes decode) reused this neutral mapping, a malformed signature would surface as canonicalization_failed rather than invalid_signature, blurring the fail-closed error taxonomy required by P-CANON-FAILCLOSED-BEFORE-SIG (canonicalization vs invalid_signature must stay distinct). The module doc (lines 7-12) explicitly instructs security callers to decode with their own mapping (crypto.rs -> InvalidSignature). This is a correct design as documented; flagging the latent footgun that the shared helper's error is only domain-appropriate for structural (hash-id) decodes, not signature/key decodes.

defer.title: Recursion-depth bound (128) on all three value paths prevents stack-overflow DoS on untrusted nesting
defer.detail: MAX_PARSE_DEPTH=128 is enforced consistently on the raw Parser (parse_value, lines 353-364), the serde converter (from_serde_value_at, lines 156/166), and the public hand-constructible serializer (write_value, lines 207/220). This closes the panic-on-untrusted-input / stack-overflow DoS vector for the public JcsValue surface and matches serde_json's default recursion_limit so raw and serde paths reject at the same depth (preserving raw-path/value-path agreement needed for hash binding). Tests at lines 814-946 cover the inclusive boundary on all three paths. Positive finding: this is the correct fail-closed-before-panic posture P-NO-PANIC-LIB requires; no unwrap/expect/panic in non-test code in any of the three files.

defer.title: signing_preimage clones the full object then mutates in place; large messages are duplicated in memory per verify
defer.detail: signing_preimage does object.clone() then strips two key sets and canonicalizes. For every request AND response the entire serde_json::Value tree is deep-cloned, and request_hash() invokes request_signing_preimage() which clones again — so producing both the preimage and its hash on the signer/verifier hot path clones the object twice. Not a correctness bug (canonicalization needs an owned, mutated copy), but worth noting as an avoidable allocation on the per-message pipeline; a strip-into-a-borrowed-serializer approach would avoid the clone. No security impact.

defer.title: strip_signature_value tolerates a missing 'value' key, silently producing a preimage from a signature block that never had a value
defer.detail: signature_obj.remove("value") ignores its return and does not distinguish 'value was present and removed' from 'value was already absent'. This is intentional and documented (the preimage is defined as 'the object with signature.value removed' regardless of prior presence), and is symmetric between signer and verifier, so it is correct for preimage construction. Flagged only to confirm the design choice is deliberate: a wire message whose signature block has no 'value' will still produce a well-formed preimage here, and rejecting that as malformed is left to the verification pipeline (required-field/format checks), not this layer. Ensure that pipeline check exists, since this function will not surface the omission.

defer.title: strip_observability_meta is silently infallible when params._meta is a non-object, while strip_signature_value already guaranteed envelope presence — consistent but means a non-object _meta value is never rejected here
defer.detail: strip_observability_meta uses .and_then(|meta| meta.as_object_mut()) and silently does nothing if _meta is not an object. Because strip_signature_value runs first and already dereferences container._meta[meta_key].signature, the located _meta must be an object for the call to reach this point, so this is not exploitable on the request/response preimage paths. The JCS value-domain / fail-closed validation (P-CANON-FAILCLOSED-BEFORE-SIG) is correctly delegated to canonicalize(raw_bytes) at pipeline.rs:164 before signature verification. Recorded as info: the no-op branch is dead on the real paths but its infallibility is by design and documented.

defer.title: verify_ed25519_with correctly uses verify_strict and maps every failure mode to the caller-chosen error with no panic path
defer.detail: Confirmed (positive): verify_ed25519_with uses ed25519_dalek verify_strict (rejects small-order/weak public keys and non-canonical R), decodes the signature with no unwrap/expect/panic, and returns the supplied error on bad base64, wrong length (try_into to [u8;64]), and cryptographic mismatch. The request wrapper maps to InvalidSignature and the response path passes ResponseSigInvalid, satisfying the distinct-token requirement. Note for scope: crypto.rs does NOT itself check alg==Ed25519 (that gate lives in pipeline check_signature_block / response step 4). The signing key path from_seed_bytes is infallible by construction and exposes no key accessor. No DoS or fail-open found in this unit.

defer.title: Freshness rejects malformed timestamps as ExpiredRequest rather than CanonicalizationFailed
defer.detail: parse_rfc3339_utc maps every malformed timestamp (bad grammar, wrong separators, out-of-range fields) to McpsError::ExpiredRequest. This is documented as a deliberate fail-closed choice and is safe (still rejects). However a structurally invalid issued_at/expires_at is arguably a format violation that the normative order would surface earlier as a field-format/canonicalization error, not as a freshness verdict. Since the freshness step runs after required-field format checks in the pipeline, a malformed timestamp that slipped past earlier format validation is reported under the wrong taxonomy token. Confirm the upstream format check rejects bad RFC3339 before step 9 so this fallback is never the surfaced verdict; if it can be, the token is mislabeled.

defer.title: InMemoryReplayCache prune boundary is consistent with inclusive freshness upper bound (no current window, but fragile to inequality changes)
defer.detail: retain_until = expires_at_unix + max_clock_skew_secs and prune evicts when retain_until < now_unix (strictly past). The freshness upper bound is expires_at + skew INCLUSIVE (time.rs accepts now <= upper). At now == expires_at+skew the request is still admissible AND the entry is retained (retain_until >= now); at retain_until+1 the entry is pruned but freshness already rejects. Boundaries are consistent and there is no current replay readmission window. Flagged only because a future change making either inequality exclusive would silently open a one-second readmission window; no bug today.

defer.title: max_clock_skew_secs not validated non-negative; a negative skew narrows replay retention (more replay admission)
defer.detail: InMemoryReplayCache::new accepts any i64 skew. A negative skew makes retain_until = expires_at_unix + (negative) < expires_at, so entries are pruned earlier than expires_at, shortening retention. If the cache and check_freshness are ever configured with different skew values, a nonce could be readmitted while a request is still within its freshness window. check_freshness documents negative skew as fail-closed-tightening, but the replay-retention direction is the opposite (shorter retention = MORE replay admission). Recommend guarding skew >= 0 at construction or documenting that one shared non-negative skew must drive both freshness and replay retention.

defer.title: Replay insert uses raw envelope.audience rather than the verified expected_audience
defer.detail: verify_request keys the replay cache with &envelope.audience (line 206). This is safe ONLY because step 8 (line 175) already rejects any request whose envelope.audience != config.expected_audience, so by line 203 the two are provably equal. The keying is therefore conformant to P-REPLAY-DETECT/P-REPLAY-AFTER-SIG, but it relies on an upstream invariant rather than passing config.expected_audience directly. If the step-8 equality check were ever weakened or reordered, the replay key would silently key on attacker-controlled audience, allowing same-signer/nonce replays across differing audience strings to be treated as Fresh. Flagging as info: currently correct, latent coupling.

defer.title: check_freshness silently accepts negative max_clock_skew without normative guard
defer.detail: check_freshness documents that a negative max_clock_skew_secs merely tightens the window and 'can only reject more, never admit more.' That reasoning is sound for freshness (still fail-closed). But the SAME max_clock_skew_secs is passed into the replay cache as the retention extension (pipeline line 184 vs the cache constructor). A negative skew there would SHORTEN retain-until below expires_at, pruning nonces while the request is still replayable within the freshness window — a replay-retention gap. Neither time.rs nor replay.rs rejects a negative skew. P-REPLAY-DETECT requires retention until expires_at + max_clock_skew. Config should constrain skew to be non-negative; currently nothing in this unit does. Info because the value is operator config, not attacker input.

defer.title: Malformed expires_at at replay step maps to ExpiredRequest, a benign taxonomy reuse
defer.detail: At step 12 the pipeline re-parses envelope.expires_at via parse_rfc3339_utc (line 202) to feed the replay cache; a parse failure here returns McpsError::ExpiredRequest. This is unreachable in practice because check_freshness at step 9 already parsed the same expires_at successfully, but the fallback token is correct and fail-closed. No leakage of internal detail into the wire token (wire.rs renders only the bare mcps.* code). Noted for completeness; no action.

defer.title: wire.rs error object uses unwrap_or_else fallback rather than unwrap — no panic on input
defer.detail: json_rpc_error_object serializes via serde_json::to_vec(&object).unwrap_or_else(...) with a literal byte fallback, satisfying P-NO-PANIC-LIB (no unwrap/expect/panic on the failure path). The 'details' field echoes the bare mcps.* wire token (not any human-readable diagnostic), so the ReplayCacheError/TrustResolverError 'details: String' payloads are never leaked to the wire — consistent with the error-taxonomy non-leakage intent. Confirmed clean.

defer.title: Replay cache failure correctly distinct from replay verdict and fails closed
defer.detail: ReplayCacheError::Unavailable maps to McpsError::ReplayCacheUnavailable via to_mcps_error and the From impl, kept distinct from ReplayDecision::Replay -> McpsError::ReplayDetected. No path maps a cache failure to Fresh/allow. Satisfies P-REPLAY-CACHE-FAILCLOSED / P-V03-REPLAY-OPERR-FAILCLOSED for the core abstraction. Positive confirmation; the actual fail-closed wiring (replay invoked LAST, only after sig verify) lives in verify and is not in this unit.

defer.title: No panic/unwrap on untrusted input across the unit (library code stays Result-based)
defer.detail: The only unwrap-family call in non-test code is unwrap_or_else (infallible, provides a fallback, never panics). time.rs, replay.rs, resolver.rs, unwrap.rs use no unwrap/expect/panic in library paths and return Result over the frozen taxonomy. Arithmetic in time.rs/replay.rs uses saturating_add/saturating_sub or operates on bounded (<=4-digit year) inputs, so untrusted-input DoS via panic or overflow is not present in this unit. Satisfies P-NO-PANIC-LIB for these files.

defer.title: unwrap.rs result reshape recovery is post-verification only and signals inner errors distinctly
defer.detail: unwrap_verified_result is documented and structured to run AFTER signature verification; it recognizes the value/inner_error wrapper ONLY when it is the sole non-_meta key (exact-key match), preventing a legitimate inner object containing a 'value' field from being misclassified, and surfaces InnerError distinctly so an inner error cannot masquerade as a JSON-RPC success. No signature/trust decision is made here (it only reshapes an already-verified payload), so no bypass surface. Confirm callers actually render InnerError as a top-level JSON-RPC error and never as result, since that discrimination is the security-relevant invariant.

defer.title: SeededNonceSource.offset increments unbounded (usize overflow after long runs)
defer.detail: `self.offset += 1;` grows without bound across draws; after usize::MAX draws it overflows (debug panic / release wrap, which also jumps the modular position). This is a test-only provider explicitly marked not-for-production and the bound is astronomically large, so impact is negligible, but the counter could use wrapping_add or modulo-on-increment for cleanliness.

defer.title: Failed response verification leaves pending entry; bounded retry until expiry (by design)
defer.detail: verify_response evicts the pending entry only on SUCCESS; a failed verification intentionally leaves it so a later correctly-bound response can verify (documented). This is the correct fail-closed choice. Residual: an attacker feeding arbitrary response bytes for a known in-flight id can repeatedly attempt verification against the stored request_hash until expire_pending/cancel_request removes the entry, but each attempt must still pass full Ed25519 verification against the trusted server key — no forgery advantage, bounded CPU only. expire_pending is caller-driven (not automatic), so a host that never calls it accumulates entries for abandoned ids (pending_count exposed for monitoring). Confirms the binding-to-STORED-hash design (never a caller-supplied expected hash) correctly upholds P-RESPONSE-HASH-BIND.

defer.title: Private signing key correctly encapsulated — no accessor, no Debug derive, signs via shared core preimage
defer.detail: Positive confirmation for the unit focus: HostSigner holds SigningKey privately with NO accessor returning the key or a detached signature (only finished wire bytes), and derives no Debug, so the key cannot leak via formatting/logging or a public API. Signing uses the SHARED mcps_core::request_signing_preimage (identical exclusions for signer and verifier: signature.value + W3C trace-context keys), Ed25519 directly with no pre-hash, Base64URL-no-pad, alg hardcoded to SIG_ALG_ED25519 — upholding P-SIGN-WHOLE-OBJECT, P-SIGN-PREIMAGE-EXCLUSIONS, P-SIGN-ALG-ED25519-ONLY, P-SIGN-ENCODING. Nonce draws NONCE_BYTES=16 (128 bits) from OS CSPRNG. No fail-open or signing-scope defect found in the host signing/verification path.

defer.title: serve_once does not enforce max_concurrent_connections (only the unused multi-threaded serve does)
defer.detail: The production binary (main.rs:418) drives the listener with serve_once in a sequential loop, which handles exactly one connection at a time and never reads options.limits.max_concurrent_connections. The cap is honored only by the separate multi-threaded serve() (tls.rs:549). This is internally consistent (single-threaded loop => effective concurrency of 1, and the RefCell replay cache mandates single-threading), but it means the documented ServerLimits.max_concurrent_connections field is silently a no-op on the production path. Not a defect, but a latent confusion: a future switch to serve() would also need the replay cache to become thread-safe. Worth a doc note that the field is inert under serve_once.

defer.title: id_value for outgoing error objects is taken from raw unverified request bytes
defer.detail: handle_with_transport parses the inbound bytes once with serde_json::from_slice(...).ok() and extracts top-level "id" for use as the JSON-RPC error id on every fail-closed path. This is correct for correlation, but note it trusts the unverified/attacker-controlled id verbatim for error responses (error responses are intentionally unsigned). That is acceptable per design (the signed success path re-derives id from the verified request at build_signed_response), but worth recording that an attacker can choose the id echoed in a fail-closed error object. No integrity impact since error objects carry no signature and no security decision keys off this id.

defer.title: Duplicate top-level keys in inbound bytes parsed multiple times with serde default last-wins for id extraction
defer.detail: handle_with_transport and build_forwarded_request each independently call serde_json::from_slice on the same request_bytes (lines 210, 234 via parsed, 294, and again on the inner response at 357). The id used for error correlation comes from the first parse using serde_json default semantics (last-wins on duplicate members), while the canonical dup-key rejection happens inside verify_request (P-CANON-DUPKEY-REJECT). Since a dup-key request fails verification and never reaches dispatch, the only externally visible effect is the echoed error id; no signed shape is built from the default-parsed value. Low risk, but the multiple independent parses of the same bytes are a minor robustness smell — any divergence between these parses and the in-core canonical parse is a place where the proxy's view could differ from the verified view.

defer.title: Transport-layer connection_rejection runs before the object-signature verify gate; verify chain still intact
defer.detail: serve_once invokes connection_rejection / routing_header_rejection (cert-lifetime, OCSP, routing-header hygiene) BEFORE calling the handler (which runs verify_request). This ordering is correct — these are transport-layer guards on the verified TLS peer, independent of the object signature, and a rejection here means the handler/inner is never reached (fail closed). The error id is read from the raw request body (unverified) which is acceptable for an unsigned transport error. No bypass: a connection that passes these guards still goes through the full verify gate in the handler. Recorded for completeness of the verify-before-dispatch chain; no defect.

defer.title: build_signed_response signs every success-shaped inner reply; only non-JSON inner bytes take the unsigned error path
defer.detail: The None arm (no 'result' member) wraps the ENTIRE inner value under inner_error and signs it, with the outgoing id taken from the verified request, not the inner id. This correctly covers a hostile inner returning a top-level error, a bare scalar/array, or a garbage object — all still signed + request-bound + id-correlated. The only inner shape that can suppress the signature is one that makes serde_json::from_slice fail (non-JSON bytes), which maps to CanonicalizationFailed and a fail-closed unsigned JSON-RPC error object via dispatch_and_sign's caller — consistent with all other failure paths. No unsigned SUCCESS shape found; the build_signed_response coverage of inner shapes is complete.

defer.title: Observability keys intentionally not part of any security decision on the forward path (conformant)
defer.detail: build_forwarded_request removes REQUEST_META_KEY/AUTHORIZATION_META_KEY/VERIFIED_META_KEY and injects fresh context, leaving caller-supplied traceparent/tracestate/baggage in params._meta to flow to the plain-MCP inner server. This is consistent with the catalog (observability keys are audit-correlation only and must not influence security decisions). Recorded as info to confirm the observability partition is respected and these keys drive no security decision in this unit.

defer.title: Verify-before-dispatch gate plus policy-deny and transport-binding all fail closed before the inner (conformant)
defer.detail: Positive confirmation: handle_with_transport routes any verify_request Err (including ReplayCacheUnavailable on a replay-cache borrow conflict at proxy.rs:224) to json_rpc_error_object WITHOUT calling self.inner.dispatch. The inner is reached only via dispatch_and_sign after verify Ok, an optional AuthorizationDecision::Deny early-return, and an optional transport_binding.check early-return — so binding runs in ADDITION to verification, never instead (P-TRANSPORT-BIND-SIGNER, P-TRANSPORT-NOT-REPLACE-SIG). connection_rejection + routing_header_rejection (tls.rs:529-534 / 593-598) likewise run before the handler. No bypass of the verify gate on any path in this unit.

defer.title: All four inner response shapes normalized into a signed, request-hash-bound envelope (conformant)
defer.detail: Positive confirmation: build_signed_response covers object result (signed in place), non-object result (wrapped under RESPONSE_WRAP_VALUE_KEY), and inner error / missing result (wrapped under RESPONSE_WRAP_INNER_ERROR_KEY); in every case it computes response_signing_preimage and signs via the ResponseSigner seam, mapping a signing failure to ResponseSigInvalid (fail closed). No path yields an unsigned top-level response from an inner-controlled payload (tests cover scalar/array/null/error). Satisfies the 'every inner shape is signed' requirement; the envelope advertises SIG_ALG_ED25519 (P-SIGN-ALG-ED25519-ONLY).

defer.title: build_forwarded_request None-arm index-assignment can clobber a non-object params defensively
defer.detail: When params._meta is not an object, the code does request["params"]["_meta"] = json!({...}) (320). serde_json index-assignment will replace a scalar/absent params with a nested object, silently discarding any non-object params value. A verified request always has an object params._meta (the envelope lives there), so this branch is unreachable for verified input; it is defensive only and never reached on the verified path. Info.

defer.title: Missing Content-Length yields an empty body delivered to verify (fails closed, not a bypass)
defer.detail: parse_content_length(...).unwrap_or(0) (656) means a request with a body but a missing/malformed Content-Length is truncated to a zero-length body (679). The empty body then fails verify_request as a parse/canonicalization failure, so no bypass results — the inner is never reached. Noting as info: behavior is fail-closed but silently drops body bytes rather than surfacing a 400-style framing error, which could complicate debugging legitimate clients.

defer.title: Client-cert lifetime enforcement defaults ON (1h) but can be disabled, weakening the v1 revocation posture
defer.detail: max_client_cert_lifetime defaults to Some(3600) (cli.rs:358) and --max-client-cert-lifetime none disables it; disabling is a WARNING by default (main.rs:121-126) and a HARD strict violation (cli.rs:911-917). With no online revocation in the default build, the cert-lifetime cap is the sole bound on a compromised client cert's exposure window; the layered defaults and strict rejection are appropriate. Info — posture is documented and strict-gated, consistent with P-TRUST-NO-CORE-REVOCATION-TRANSPORT (rotation/short-lifetime rather than CRL/OCSP in core).

defer.title: strip_optional_quotes removes mismatched outer quotes, not only a matched pair
defer.detail: strip_optional_quotes strips a leading '"' and a trailing '"' independently via strip_prefix then strip_suffix; it does not verify the two quotes are a genuine matched enclosing pair. For input like "a"b" it yields a"b. Envoy only emits properly-quoted values so this is not exploitable in practice, but the helper's contract ("strip a single pair of surrounding quotes") is stricter than its behavior.

defer.title: XFCC empty-CN parity asymmetry: Subject DN with empty CN fails closed here, but direct-TLS path would accept an empty CN identity
defer.detail: parse_xfcc rejects a present-but-empty selected field (extracted.is_empty() -> return None), and common_name_from_rfc2253 returns Some("") for CN= with empty value, which the caller then rejects. By contrast the direct-TLS CnLegacy path (tls.rs extract_identity, lines 308-317) maps an empty CN string straight into TransportIdentity { value: "" } with no emptiness check, so the SAME degenerate certificate (empty CN) resolves to None over XFCC but to an empty-string identity over direct TLS. This contradicts the module's stated cross-strategy parity goal (same cert -> same identity). Degenerate input, but it is a documented invariant the code does not fully uphold; the direct path should also reject an empty CN.

defer.title: validate_routing_headers re-reads value via first().unwrap_or("") after counting, dead defensive branch plus double walk
defer.detail: In the count()==1 branch, headers.first(header).unwrap_or("") substitutes an empty string if first() returns None. count()==1 guarantees first() is Some, so unwrap_or("") is dead defensive code; if it ever did fire, an empty value fails validate_asserted_identity_value as Empty (fail-closed), so it is harmless. Noted as minor redundancy (two passes over the header list per routing header) and a defensive branch that could mask a future logic change.

defer.title: `RequestHeaders::first` first-wins on duplicates is safe for the binding header but routing-header dup-detection depends on a separate count() call ordering
defer.detail: The binding provider correctly fails closed when `count(header_name) != 1` (line 443) BEFORE reading `first`, so a duplicated trust header cannot smuggle. `validate_routing_headers` (line 257) likewise checks `count` per header and rejects `_ => Duplicate`. Both are correct. Noting for completeness that the safety of `first`-wins (line 128) is entirely contingent on every security-relevant caller pre-checking `count`; there is no type-level guarantee a future caller won't read `first` on a possibly-duplicated header. No current defect.

defer.title: Transport binding is additional hardening and does not substitute for signature/authz — correctly ordered
defer.detail: Confirmed conformance for P-TRANSPORT-NOT-REPLACE-SIG and P-TRANSPORT-BIND-SIGNER: in proxy.rs the binding `check(&verified.verified_signer, transport_identity)` runs AFTER `verify_request` (object signature, freshness, replay) and AFTER Phase-5 authorization, before dispatch (lines 227-255). A mismatch or absent-required identity yields `McpsError::TransportBindingFailed` and the inner server is never reached. ExactMatchBinding/MappedBinding both fail closed on `identity == None` (lines 597-602, 637-643). No fail-open path found in this unit.

defer.title: Asserted-identity length bound is applied to char-trimmed string length, not raw byte/element budget; XFCC whole-header is unbounded
defer.detail: validate_asserted_identity_value bounds the trimmed PLAIN value and each extracted XFCC field to MAX_ASSERTED_IDENTITY_LEN (8192). However the XFCC path (parse_xfcc) tokenizes and DN-parses the ENTIRE raw header value before any per-field length check, and the whole-header size is bounded only by whatever the upstream HTTP header parser permits — split_xfcc_pairs, common_name_from_rfc2253, and split_unescaped all allocate Vecs over the full input. ADR-MCPS-023 requires asserted-identity metadata be length-bounded to bound parse/log cost; a multi-megabyte XFCC header with many quoted elements is parsed in full before the per-field bound rejects it. Low DoS surface given a trusted upstream, but the length-bound property is enforced on the field, not on the parse-cost-driving raw input.

defer.title: RequestHeaders::parse uses str::lines() which treats lone \n as a line break, diverging from HTTP CRLF framing
defer.detail:
parse splits the header block with header_block.lines(), which breaks on both 
 and 
. A value smuggled with a bare 
 that survived upstream framing would be split into a spurious header line here rather than retained in-value. This particular module is defended downstream because validate_asserted_identity_value rejects control chars in the trusted-header value, but parse is the general header view fed to count()/first() and to routing-header validation, where a bare-LF-injected line could register as an additional/spurious header. Since trusted-header dup detection relies on count() seeing exactly the upstream-set occurrences, a parser that invents header lines from embedded LFs is a latent header-smuggling consideration. Informational because the upstream HTTP layer is expected to have already rejected bare-LF framing and the asserted/routing validators reject control chars.

defer.title: is_transient_connection_error treats is_unrecoverable_error() as a reconnect-worthy transient trigger
defer.detail: is_transient_connection_error (line 297-302) returns true for error.is_unrecoverable_error(), classifying an 'unrecoverable' redis-rs error as transient and thus eligible for the single M19 reconnect-and-retry. Semantically an unrecoverable error is the opposite of transient; retrying it wastes one bounded reconnect. Behaviorally harmless — the worst case is one extra bounded reconnect that fails and then fails closed as Unavailable — but the naming/intent mismatch could mislead future maintenance (e.g. a WRONGTYPE-class error that redis-rs happens to mark unrecoverable would needlessly reconnect rather than surface immediately as Fatal). Low-risk correctness/clarity note.

defer.title: bounded_connect abandons (leaks) the watchdog thread and its in-flight connection on deadline timeout
defer.detail: bounded_connect (line 235-281) spawns a detached thread to run the blocking connect+handshake and, on rx.recv_timeout deadline elapse (line 274), returns Unavailable while the spawned thread remains blocked on the dead socket forever (documented at line 242-244 as intentional). Each timed-out connect/reconnect therefore leaks one OS thread plus its half-open socket/Client until process exit. For a single startup connect this is negligible, but because every subsequent op can trigger an M19 reconnect through this same path (run_with_reconnect -> bounded_connect), a backend that repeatedly accepts TCP then stalls the handshake will leak one thread per failed reconnect attempt over the process lifetime. The thread cannot corrupt shared state (it owns only its doomed connection), so this is a slow resource leak under sustained adversarial/sinkhole conditions, not a safety bug.

defer.title: P-V03-REPLAY-TTL-ROUNDUP: TTL is clamped/floored to minimal 1ms but seconds granularity is rounded DOWN, not UP, when sub-second precision is lost
defer.detail: compute_ttl_ms computes ttl_secs = (expires_at_unix - now_unix).max(0) then *1000. The property requires TTLs computed from remaining validity be rounded UP, never down. Here both expires_at and now are integer Unix seconds so there is no sub-second remainder to round, and the result is exact; the +max_clock_skew fold and the .max(1) floor both bias toward retaining longer. The non-positive-remaining case is also handled: a stale request still records a 1ms sighting rather than being rejected before the store, which differs slightly from the property's 'reject before the shared store is consulted' for non-positive remaining TTL — but freshness rejection happens earlier in the pipeline (not in this unit), so this is not a true violation. Flagging as info for traceability that the round-up direction is satisfied only because of integer-seconds inputs; if expires_at ever becomes sub-second this floor-then-truncate would round down.

defer.title: shared_remote_signer multiple-active check is enforced only at construction, not at resolve time, so a key-set mutated after construction could admit a second active key under shared mode
defer.detail: shared_remote_signer() verifies active_count_for(audience) <= 1 at construction (line 292) and resolve() additionally restricts admission to the single configured shared_key_id (lines 340-344), so even if the underlying AuthorizedKeySet gained a second active key, resolve would still reject anything other than shared_key_id. The set is owned by value and has no mutation API, so this is not currently exploitable; noting for completeness that the one-active-key invariant is a construction-time assertion while the actual runtime admission gate is the shared_key_id equality check, which is the stronger and load-bearing one. No conformance gap.

defer.title: valid_from/valid_until validity window uses verify-time clock but does not incorporate clock skew, unlike the replay/freshness paths
defer.detail: KeySetTrustResolver::resolve checks now < valid_from -> NotFound and now > valid_until -> NotFound with no skew tolerance, whereas the replay caches fold max_clock_skew into retain_until. For publish-before-use rotation (P-V03-ROTATION-PUBLISH-BEFORE-USE) the operator waits >= T before valid_from, so a small skew at the boundary could transiently reject a freshly-valid key (fail-closed, the safe direction) or, at valid_until, reject slightly early. This is the conservative direction (never admits an out-of-window key early/late), so it does not violate any MUST; noting that the admission window is skew-tight while the replay window is skew-loose, which is a deliberate and safe asymmetry.

defer.title: KeySetTrustResolver validity window omits clock skew on the server-key admission bounds
defer.detail: resolve() rejects with NotFound when `now < valid_from` or `now > valid_until` using an exact comparison with no clock-skew tolerance. For response-signing key admission this is the conservative (fail-closed) direction at both ends — a freshly-valid or just-expired key is denied rather than admitted — so it is not a security weakness. Noting it only because a node whose clock lags a publisher by more than the publish-before-use propagation could transiently deny a legitimately rotated key (an availability, not security, concern). No action required for the security lens.

defer.title: WAIT-quorum insufficient-acks correctly fails closed and is non-retryable; verified no fresh-on-outage path
defer.detail: Confirmation (not a defect): on a REDIS_WAIT_QUORUM fresh insert, an insufficient WAIT ack count or a WAIT error is mapped to OpAttempt::Fatal (not Transient), so run_with_reconnect does NOT retry — correctly avoiding the non-idempotency trap where the retried SET NX would see its own just-written key and wrongly report Replay. Both paths surface ReplayStoreError::Unavailable -> ReplayCacheError::Unavailable -> mcps.replay_cache_unavailable. This satisfies P-V03-REPLAY-OPERR-FAILCLOSED and P-V03-REPLAY-TIER-CEILING. Included as an explicit positive so the verifier need not re-derive it.

defer.title: Error diagnostics embed redis/connection error strings but no nonce/key material; startup audit line scrubbed of nonce
defer.detail: Confirmation (not a defect): ReplayStoreError::Unavailable details include the underlying redis error text (`redis SET NX PX failed: {e}`) and the composite key is never logged — the SET arg is the already-hashed composite_key, and WAIT/connection errors carry only counts/timeouts. The replay_tier startup_audit_line explicitly asserts (and tests) that no 'nonce' substring leaks. No secret/nonce/private-key leakage via errors or logs observed in this unit. Included so the verifier can close the error-leak lens for these files.

defer.title: close_fds_above_stdio SAFETY/doc comment understates the syscalls it makes (getrlimit, not only close)
defer.detail: The doc and the SAFETY comment on apply_close_extra_fds/close_fds_above_stdio state the post-fork/pre-exec hook 'issues ONLY close(2) syscalls' / 'calls only getrlimit/sysconf for the upper bound and close(2)'. The two are inconsistent (one says only close, the other adds getrlimit); the code does call libc::getrlimit before the close loop. getrlimit IS on the POSIX async-signal-safe list so the hook is sound, but the 'ONLY close(2)' phrasing in apply_close_extra_fds (line 474-475) is inaccurate. Tighten the comment so a future auditor verifying async-signal-safety isn't misled about which syscalls actually run in the child.

defer.title: close_fds_above_stdio can close fds an async-signal-unsafe-built earlier pre_exec hook expects, if registration order assumption is ever violated
defer.detail: Correctness of the fd-close hook depends entirely on it being registered LAST (after setrlimit and the Landlock/seccomp sandbox hooks), documented at line 467-469 and enforced only by call-order in persistent_inner.rs (apply_rlimits, apply_sandbox, then apply_close_extra_fds). The Landlock sandbox builds a ruleset fd in the parent and the enforce closure uses it in the child; if a future caller reorders these (or adds a new fd-holding pre_exec hook after close_extra_fds), the close loop would close the ruleset fd (fd>=3) before restrict_self runs, breaking enforcement. This is a fragile ordering invariant with no compile-time guard. Not a current bug (order is correct at the one call site) but worth a guard or a single combined apply method.

defer.title: raw_ed25519_point only accepts short-form DER OCTET STRING wrapper; a long-form-encoded 32-byte point is rejected
defer.detail: raw_ed25519_point accepts either bare 32 bytes or exactly 34 bytes of the form 0x04 0x20 <32 bytes> (short-form DER OCTET STRING). PKCS#11 modules returning CKA_EC_POINT wrapped with a redundant long-form length (e.g. 0x04 0x81 0x20 ...) would be rejected as malformed. This is fail-closed (rejects rather than mis-parses), so it is at most an interop limitation with a non-conformant token, not a security bug; flagged for completeness against the documented 'accept both' intent.

defer.title: is_fresh lower-bound uses saturating_add(skew) which silently widens the accept window at the u64/Duration ceiling
defer.detail: is_fresh computes `now_unix.saturating_add(skew) < this_update` for the lower bound and `now_unix > next.saturating_add(skew)` for the upper. saturating_add cannot overflow in practice for realistic SystemTime/thisUpdate values, but if a responder supplied an absurd nextUpdate near Duration::MAX the saturating_add would clamp and the comparison would still behave (clamping only makes the window narrower at the top / wider only at extreme values that cannot represent real time). No exploitable freshness bypass found; noting that the arithmetic is saturating rather than checked so a pathological GeneralizedTime cannot panic — which is the desired fail-safe behavior.

defer.title: EnvKeySource remove_var is process-global and racy under threads (dev-only, feature-gated)
defer.detail: EnvKeySource::read calls std::env::remove_var after reading the seed/PEM, to shrink the window the secret is inheritable. std::env::remove_var is process-global and is not thread-safe with concurrent getenv in other threads (a known unsoundness, now unsafe in Rust 2024). This type is gated behind the non-default dev_env_key_source feature and is documented as DEV/CI-only, never in production, and the value is held in Zeroizing. Impact is therefore confined to dev builds. Informational: the seed is correctly never placed in error payloads (errors carry only the var NAME), so no key leak via errors here.

defer.title: FileKeySource/EnvKeySource re-read and re-derive the signing key from disk on EVERY response sign
defer.detail: FileKeySource::sign_response (and EnvKeySource::sign_response) call self.signing_key()? on every invocation, which re-reads the seed file from disk, decodes Base64URL, and rebuilds the dalek key per signed response. This is correct for the zeroize hygiene goal (seed temporaries scrubbed each call) and not a defect, but it makes per-response signing latency/availability hostage to filesystem read throughput and repeatedly pages the raw seed into memory — a wider secret-exposure window and a small DoS-amplification surface for a sidecar that signs every response. The Pkcs11KeySource path deliberately amortizes its login (M16) for exactly this reason; the file/env paths do not amortize the load. Noted as a hygiene/availability asymmetry, not a leak.

defer.title: OCSP responder ByName identity match compares raw DER encodings, not normalized DNs
defer.detail: responder_id_matches() for ResponderId::ByName compares the DER of the responder_id Name against the DER of the signer cert subject byte-for-byte (a == b). Identity is ultimately bound by the prior signature verification (verify_responder_signature returns the signer whose key verified the response), so this byName check is a secondary belt-and-suspenders bind and fails CLOSED on any encoding difference (rejecting a legitimately-matching-but-differently-encoded name rather than admitting a wrong one). Direction is safe; flagged only because byte-equal DN comparison can reject valid responders whose DN re-encoding differs (string-type or attribute-order variance), an availability edge, never an admit-on-mismatch.

defer.title: PKCS#11 sign/attribute buffers are sized directly from token-reported lengths with no sanity ceiling
defer.detail: sign_eddsa_raw allocates vec![0u8; sig_len] and get_ec_point_raw allocates vec![0u8; len] using the CK_ULONG length the token returns from the two-call length idiom, the only guard being the post-fill 'written > buffer.len()' protocol check and (for EC_POINT) a CK_ULONG::MAX sentinel reject. A malicious or buggy module reporting a multi-gigabyte length would trigger a large allocation before the 64-byte Ed25519 validity check (which happens at the keysource layer, pkcs11_keysource.rs:491, AFTER the wrapper allocates). The token is in the trusted custody base so this is informational, but a small upper bound on the requested length (a signature/point is tens of bytes) would harden the wrapper against a misbehaving module.

defer.title: Argument-scope constraint is a subset match: request arguments beyond the constrained keys are unconstrained
defer.detail: arguments_satisfied only checks that every key named in the grant's `arguments` constraint equals the request's corresponding value (`actual.get(key) == Some(value)`). Request arguments NOT named in the constraint are permitted with any value, and extra keys never cause a deny. For a grant intended to pin an exact argument set this is broader than it looks; whether subset semantics are intended should be made explicit, since a grant constraining `{path: "/safe"}` still authorizes a call that additionally passes `{path: "/safe", recurse: true, force: true}`.

defer.title: Manifest validity window allows now == expires_at / issued_at boundaries inconsistent with documented '[issued_at, expires_at]' but doc-comment says 'at or before'
defer.detail: check_window uses `now < issued_at` and `now > expires_at` as the reject conditions, i.e. the window is inclusive [issued_at, expires_at]. The ManifestError::ManifestExpired doc-comment (manifest_error.rs line 57) instead says 'expires_at is at or before the supplied now' (exclusive). The code (inclusive) and the error doc (exclusive) disagree by one boundary second. Not a security hole (the reference profile's check_window in reference.rs uses the same inclusive convention), but the contradictory documentation should be reconciled so the boundary semantics are unambiguous.

defer.title: DeadlineStream::flush does not enforce the aggregate handshake deadline
defer.detail: DeadlineStream guards read() and write() with check_deadline(), but flush() delegates straight to the inner stream with no deadline check. rustls' complete_io drives I/O primarily through read/write so the practical exposure is small, but a peer that stalls specifically during a flush of buffered handshake bytes would not be cut off by the aggregate wall-clock deadline that the MCPS-094 guard was added to enforce. The per-socket write timeout still bounds each individual flush, so this is a completeness gap in the aggregate-deadline guard, not an unbounded stall.

defer.title: Validity-window malformed-timestamp handling collapses to AuthorizationExpired rather than a malformed token
defer.detail: check_window maps a parse failure of not_before/expires_at to PolicyError::AuthorizationExpired (fail closed) rather than AuthorizationMalformed. Behaviorally fine (deny either way), but a malformed timestamp is arguably an artifact-shape defect, not an expiry. No security impact — the request is denied. Reporting as info for token-precision conformance with the freshness/window properties.

defer.title: wire.rs error fallback emits a JSON-RPC error with id:null on serialization failure, but the primary path cannot realistically fail
defer.detail: json_rpc_authorization_error builds a fixed-shape object from a static wire_code string and an id Value, then serde_json::to_vec with an unwrap_or_else fallback. The fallback never leaks artifact bytes or PolicyError context (it embeds only the static wire token, which is already public), and avoids a panic (P-NO-PANIC-LIB compliant). No issue; documenting that the fail-closed denial envelope correctly carries retryable:false and the bare mcps.authorization_* token only.

defer.title: Client mTLS transport correctly fails closed on absent server CA and uses WebPkiServerVerifier, not accept-any (except behind off-by-default fault feature)
defer.detail: ClientTlsConfig::from_der returns EmptyServerCa when the server-CA bundle is empty (server authentication is mandatory, fail closed) and builds a standard WebPkiServerVerifier bound to the configured roots; MtlsClient pins the expected ServerName and drives complete_io to authenticate the server BEFORE writing the request body, so a wrong-CA/wrong-identity/expired server cert aborts the handshake and the body never reaches the wire. The accept-any verifier exists only under cfg(feature="fault_accept_any_server"), explicitly off by default and excluded from the default test build — a deliberate fault-injection control, not a default fail-open. This satisfies P-TRANSPORT-BIND-SIGNER's transport-identity-confusion concern at the client side and P-CLAIM-CEILING (no accept-any in the shipped path). No defect found; recorded as positive confirmation for this lens.

defer.title: Authorization evaluation order differs from the ADR-MCPS-013 normative ordering (signature checked before grantee/subject/audience bindings)
defer.detail: P-AUTHZ-BINDINGS specifies evaluate order grantee==signer, subject==on_behalf_of, audience, validity window, revocation, then scope. The reference profile instead verifies the issuer signature FIRST (verify_issuer_signature, line 139) and only then checks grantee/subject/audience/window/revocation/scope (lines 142-165). All checks are still performed and all fail closed, so this is not a bypass; it is an ordering divergence from the catalog's stated sequence and only affects which mcps.authorization_* token a caller sees first. Flagged as info for ordering-conformance review.

defer.title: HTTP response body extraction returns the entire buffer when no header terminator is found
defer.detail: extract_body searches for the first CRLFCRLF and, if absent, returns the whole response buffer as the body (unwrap_or(0)). A malformed/short response (e.g. status line only, or a peer that closes before sending headers) is handed back to the caller as opaque bytes that include HTTP framing. Downstream object-signature/JCS verification will reject such bytes, so this is not a security bypass, but it conflates no body with whole-buffer body and could surface confusing errors. Info-level robustness note; consider returning empty bytes (or an error) when the terminator is absent.

defer.title: Non-DNS/non-IP expected server names silently fall back to a 'localhost' Host header
defer.detail: server_name_host maps any ServerName variant that is neither DnsName nor IpAddress to the literal localhost for the HTTP Host header. Server IDENTITY verification is correctly performed by rustls against self.server_name (not the Host header), so this does not weaken transport authentication or cause identity confusion at the TLS layer. The fallback only affects the application-layer Host header value for exotic ServerName variants. Info-level; harmless given MtlsClient::with_limits only accepts names parseable by ServerName::try_from, but the catch-all could mask a future ServerName variant.

defer.title: fault_accept_any_server feature compiles an accept-any server verifier that fully neuters server trust/identity/expiry
defer.detail: Under cfg(feature = fault_accept_any_server) the WebPkiServerVerifier is discarded and replaced by AcceptAnyServerVerifier (lib.rs:603-613), which returns ServerCertVerified::assertion() unconditionally, accepting any CA, identity, and validity window. This is intentional fault-injection (test of the tests, MCPS-071), documented as off by default and never enabled in production or default bazel test, and the default code path is the byte-for-byte verifying branch. Recorded as info so an auditor confirms the feature gate is never enabled by any shipped/Bazel target and cannot be turned on transitively via a workspace default-features path.

defer.title: Reference artifact validity-window check uses no clock skew tolerance
defer.detail: check_window enforces strict now_unix < not_before || now_unix > expires_at with no skew allowance. P-FRESHNESS-WINDOW (Core request envelope) mandates a +/- skew window, but that governs Core request freshness, not the authorization artifact's own validity window, which has no normative skew requirement in the catalog. Behaviour is fail-closed (a clock a few seconds off rejects rather than over-accepts), so this is conservative and correct; noted only so reviewers confirm artifact-window skew is intentionally absent and consistent with how the host mints not_before/expires_at.
